segments.s665-g121: برین = بر + این (poetic orthography) meaning “over/through this”; it precedes the noun it modifies (پادشاهی). پادشاهی is the governed noun “kingdom/kingship.” بَخواهم گذشت is a classical/future construction: بَ‑خواهَم (auxiliary indicating will/desire) + گذشت (the verb ‘pass’) = “I will pass.” Word order is literary: preposition + demonstrative + noun, then the verb cluster.
segments.s665-g122: بدریا is a fused adverbial phrase “to/into the sea” (بد + ریا/دریا in older spelling) indicating direction. سپاهست is سپاه + است contracted into one word meaning “there is an army” (nominal sentence); the copula is expressed inside this form. و بر کوه و دشت is a coordinated locative phrase “and on mountain and plain,” with the verb/copula understood from سپاهست (ellipsis of repeated 'is').
segments.s665-g123: چو here = “when.” The verb برخواند (بر + خواند) means “read aloud” or “read.” The object آن نامه را (that letter + object marker را) appears before the subject شهریار — a literary word order where the verb comes earlier and the subject follows the object. Overall: “When the king read that letter…”
segments.s665-g124: بپژمرد is a past/result verb meaning “he withered/grew faint/was struck down” (classical poetic usage). زان = ز + آن meaning “from/at that” and introduces the cause or source (“because of that”); لشکر بی‌شمار = “a countless host.” The clause thus states the result (he withered) caused by that countless army.
segments.s665-g125: وزان = و + زان “and from that / thereupon,” a connective linking to the previous event. روی here functions in the idiom از آن روی / وزان روی = “thereupon” (i.e. indicating consequence/sequence). قیصر is the subject “Caesar,” and بیامد is the past verb “came.” ز روم = “from Rome.” Word order is literary but straightforward: temporal/connective + subject + verb + source.
segments.s665-g126: به لشکر here introduces the force (literally “with/into an army” in older/poetic usage). بزیر اندر آورد is a compound of direction and verb: بزیر = “downwards,” اندر = “into/within,” آورد (آورَد) = “brought.” بوم = “land, homeland” is the goal. Together: “(he) brought (an) army down into the land.” The copula/agent (Caesar) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s665-g127: سپه بود رومی = literal order “army was Roman” (سپه = army; بود = was; رومی = Roman/Byzantine). عدد صد هزار is an appositive numeric phrase “numbering one hundred thousand” (عدد = number/numbering; صد هزار = one hundred thousand) placed after the predicate in the poetic register.
segments.s665-g128: سواران جنگ‌آور و نامدار is a noun phrase describing the army’s troops: سواران = “horsemen/riders” (subject/continuation), جنگ‌آور = “warlike/warriors,” و = “and,” نامدار = “renowned.” The copula is omitted (typical in poetry), so these adjectives qualify the horsemen mentioned.
segments.s665-g129: This is a prepositional phrase headed by the poetic preposition ز (from) followed by شهری (a city). که introduces a relative clause modifying شهری, and بگرفت is a past-tense, 3rd‑person singular perfective verb meaning “he seized/captured.” نوشین‌روان is a proper name (an epithet) placed at the end of the clause as its subject. Word order: preposition + noun + relative که + verb + subject (poetic ordering).
segments.s665-g130: که begins another relative/explanatory clause. از here means “from/of” in the sense ‘by/from his name’; نام او بود is a copular clause with بود as past tense “was.” قیصر نوان is a titular phrase (Caesar Nawan) that functions as the predicate noun. Overall: “who, by his name, was Caesar Nawan.”
segments.s665-g131: بیامد is a past tense verb “came.” ز هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase “from every country/realm” (ز + determiner هر + adjectival/relational کشوری). لشکری is an indefinite noun meaning “an army/troops” and is the grammatical object/subject of بیامد (poetic word order places the verb before its noun).
segments.s665-g132: به پیش اندرون literally “into the presence/forepart of the inner (court),” with به پیش functioning as a directional phrase and اندرون specifying the interior (palace). نامور مهتری are descriptive postmodifiers: نامور (renowned) and مهتری (chief/foremost) qualify the place or the host in idiomatic, compact poetic order. The whole segment gives the destination and character of the arrival.
segments.s665-g133: سپاهی is a collective noun ‘a force/host.’ بیامد is past “came.” ز راه خزر is a compound prepositional phrase “by/along the Khazar route” (ز + راه + proper name خزر). The sequence gives means/route of arrival.
segments.s665-g134: کز is a contracted form meaning “from/through which” and introduces a result clause. ایشان is the pronoun “they/them” (referring to that force). سیه شد is a simple past copular/become construction “became dark/black.” همه بوم و بر is an idiomatic pair meaning ‘the whole land and its habitations/people’; word order is emphatic (result + affected domain).
segments.s665-g135: جهان‌دیده is an adjective/nickname ‘world‑seen’ (worldly/experienced) modifying the proper name بدال. در پیش بود is a locative/copular phrase: در پیش = ‘in front / in the presence (of someone)’ and بود is past “was/stood.” The sequence describes someone (Badal) standing in the front/forecourt.
segments.s665-g136: که introduces an explanatory clause: با گنج و با لشکر خویش بود = ‘he was with his treasure and with his army.’ با + noun gives accompaniment; خویش is expressed analytically as خوی + ش (kinship/root + pronominal clitic), meaning “his own.” بود is the past copula. This clause qualifies the person introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s665-g177: ز راه خراسان بود رنج ما = literal order is 'from road Khorasan was suffering we'. ز + noun phrase introduces a source ('from the Khorasan road'). بود is the past copula 'was' placed before the predicate (رنج ما) in a poetic inversion; رنج ما is the nominal predicate 'our suffering'.
segments.s665-g178: که ویران کند لشکر و گنج ما = relative/subordinate clause introduced by که ('which/that'). کند is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'to do/make' used here as 'will lay waste' or 'may lay waste' in narrative; ویران modifies the understood object (it will make [them] ruined). The coordination لشکر و گنج ما lists the things threatened ('our army and our treasure').
segments.s665-g179: چو ترک اندر آید ز جیحون به جنگ = چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. ترک is subject 'the Turk'; اندر آید (an older/poetic ordering of اندر + آید) means 'enters/comes in'. ز جیحون marks origin 'from the Jihun' and به جنگ 'into battle' indicates direction/purpose.
segments.s665-g180: نباید برین کار کردن درنگ = literal order puts the negative impersonal نباید ('one must not / it must not be') first. برین is the fused form of بر + این ('on/over this' = 'in this [matter]'). کار کردن is the infinitival phrase 'to do/undertake the work', and درنگ 'delay' appears at the end; together the line means 'we must not delay in doing this work.'
segments.s665-g181: به موبد چنین گفت جوینده راه = 'به موبد' is the indirect object 'to the mobad'. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here placed before the speaker phrase as a poetic inversion), and جوینده راه is the speaker 'the seeker of the road' (subject).
segments.s665-g182: که اکنون چه سازیم با ساوه شاه = که introduces the reported question/statement. اکنون 'now' and چه 'what' start the interrogative. سازیم is the present/subjunctive form of ساختن used here as 'shall we do/what shall we do' (optative/request for advice). با + ساوه شاه means 'with the king of Saveh' (i.e. 'what shall we do about the king of Saveh?').
segments.s665-g183: بدو گفت موبد که لشکر بساز = بدو 'to him' (prepositional phrase) comes first. گفت here is 'said' (reporting verb). موبد is the speaker 'the mobad'. که introduces the imperative content. لشکر بساز is an imperative construction: لشکر (object) + بساز (second-person singular imperative 'make/raise'), meaning 'raise an army.'
segments.s665-g184: که خسرو به لشکر بود سرفراز = the clause gives the reason: که 'for/because'. خسرو is the proper name. به لشکر بود uses به + noun + بود to mean 'was in the host/army' (poetic ordering), and سرفراز is a predicate adjective 'illustrious, victorious' describing Khosrow.
segments.s665-g145: بیامد سپه is a verb–subject sequence meaning ‘the army came’ (past tense bi-āmad). تا به آب فرات functions as a destination/adverbial phrase: تا = 'as far as' and به marks direction/toward, so تا به آب فرات = 'as far as the Euphrates'. The poet uses slightly inverted order (verb before subject and then the adverbial).
segments.s665-g146: نماند is the negative past of ماند (here ‘did not remain / there was none left’). اندر آن بوم جای نبات is a locative phrase: اندر = 'in/within', آن بوم = 'that land/homeland', جای نبات = 'place for plants/vegetation'. Together: 'there remained no place for vegetation in that land.'
segments.s665-g147: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'. تاریک شد is 'became dark' (تاریک + شد). روزگار بهی is the noun plus adjective 'the good/excellent days' (بهی modifying روزگار); the clause reads literally 'when dark became the excellent days' — poetic word order places the verb between adjective and noun but the meaning is 'when the good times turned dark.'
segments.s665-g148: ز لشکر = 'from the army' (ز = 'from' in poetic register, modifying آگهی). رسید آگهی = 'news/information reached' (رسید = 'reached/arrived'). بهرمز is the recipient (Bahramz). The clause therefore means 'news from the army reached Bahramz.'
segments.s665-g149: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is 3rd-person past 'he heard'. گفتار is the object 'speech/words', and کارآگهان (literally 'those aware of the matter' or 'informants') is the genitive/possessive specifying whose words they were: 'the words of the informants.' Note the lack of an explicit ezāfe; this is normal in Ferdowsi's poetic style.
segments.s665-g150: بپژمرد is an intransitive past-form verb 'he withered/grew pale' (ب- prefix in classical Persian marking past or simple action). شاداب شاه جهان is the noun phrase 'the lively/vigorous king of the world' (شاداب modifying شاه جهان). The verb precedes the subject here, a common poetic inversion: 'the king, once vigorous, withered.'
segments.s665-g151: فرستاد is 'he sent' (implying messengers). و links actions ('and'), ایرانیان را with the object marker را marks 'the Iranians' as the direct object, and بخواند (classical past form) means 'he summoned/called.' So the sequence gives two related actions: he sent and he summoned the Iranians.
segments.s665-g152: سراسر همه کاخ = 'throughout all the palaces' or 'all across the palace(s)' (سراسر intensifies extent, همه = all). مردم is the people (direct object), and نشاند is an archaic/transitive past 'he seated [them]' (caused to sit). Word order places the locative/adverbial before the object and verb, but the meaning is 'he seated the people throughout the palaces.'
segments.s665-g73: به ره بر = a prepositional phrase placed before the noun: به + ره + بر gives a locative sense 'on/along the road.' یکی رز is an indefinite noun phrase ('a certain rose'), and پر از + noun is the classical Persian pattern 'full of ...'. دید is the simple past (3rd singular) 'he saw' and finishes the clause.
segments.s665-g74: بفرمود is an honorific/past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate/clausal complement ('that/so that') stating the commanded action. کهتر here denotes the younger/inferior person and اندر دوید is a past-tense verb phrase 'ran in/among (it/them),' with اندر marking the direction/place.
segments.s665-g75: ازان خوشه 'from that cluster' (ازان = 'from that/of that'); چند is an indefinite quantity 'a few/several.' ببرید is the verb in past 'he cut off/took' (here 'cut off' from a cluster); و برد continues the action 'and carried/took (them) away.'
segments.s665-g76: This clause lists recipients/places: بایوان (an archaic/uncertain term, likely 'pavilion' or place) و خوالیگرش (an obscure title/name) را marks the following as the definite object, and سپرد is the past 'he entrusted/handed over.' The object marker را shows these nouns receive the action of سپرد.
segments.s665-g77: بیامد is the past tense 'he came.' خداوندش is the subject 'his lord/master' with the possessive suffix -ش. اندر زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at/in that time' (an adverbial of time) following the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s665-g78: بدان مرد گفت is 'he said to that man' where the older form here is expressed by ب + دان (together functioning as 'to that') plus مرد (that man) and گفت (said). ای is the vocative 'O', followed by a two-part epithet بد بدگمان 'bad/ill—suspicious' addressing the man; بد modifies بدگمان as recrimination.
segments.s665-g79: نگهبان این رز is the noun phrase 'guardian/keeper of this rose.' نبودی is the past negative copula 'you were not' (classical 2nd person past form). به رنج uses به to mark manner/means ('by/through hardship'), so the clause says 'you were not the keeper by toil.'
segments.s665-g80: This is a correlative negation with نه ... نه = 'neither ... nor.' نه دینار دادی بها را = 'you did not give a dinar as its price' (دادی = past 'you gave'); بها را is the definite object 'the price.' نه گنج = 'nor (any) treasure.' Word order puts the quantity/thing before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s665-g9: 'ز خوی بد آید همه بدتری' is a compact proverb-like clause. ز preposition 'from' introduces cause ('from a bad disposition'). آید is a present/general verb 'comes' used proverbially. همه بدتری is a nominal phrase 'an altogether worse (thing)' — poetic inversion places the predicate after the verb, so literally 'from a bad disposition comes an all-worse.'
segments.s665-g10: نگر is an imperative 'look!/heed!' introducing the admonition. تا here has the purposive/limiting sense 'so that' (often used like 'lest' in older Persian). سوی خوی بد is the directional phrase 'toward the bad disposition.' ننگری is the negative present/subjunctive (ن- + نظری) meaning 'do not look' (2nd person singular), completing the purpose: 'heed, so that you do not look toward a bad disposition.'
segments.s665-g11: وزان پس = 'and from that/after that' (وزان = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'). نبد is an archaic negative copula 'was not' (negated past of بودن). زندگانیش here functions as 'his life/existence' (poetic form), and خوش is the predicate 'pleasant/happy.' Together: 'after that his life was not pleasant.'
segments.s665-g12: This is a causal/result clause: ز تیمار 'from worry/solicitude' gives the cause, زد is the past verb 'struck' (to hit/afflict), بر دل خویش = 'upon his own heart' (بر = on/upon; دل = heart; خویش is the reflexive 'his own' formed with خوی + ش), and تش is the afflicted state 'fever/ardor.' Poetic word order places زد before its affected noun: 'from anxiety a fever struck upon his heart.'
segments.s665-g13: بسالی is a temporal phrase 'for a year' (preposed time expression). باصطخر is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'with pride/boastfully' modifying بودی, the past 2nd-person singular 'you were.' دو ماه begins the next temporal span ('two months') that continues into the following segment.
segments.s665-g14: که here introduces a subordinate/time clause 'when/that.' کوتاه بودی = 'you were short/brief' (or 'the nights were brief' in context — classical Persian sometimes uses adjectival phrases elliptically). شبان سیاه: شبان is the plural/poetic word for 'nights' and سیاه 'black/dark' qualifies them. The clause reads roughly 'for two months the nights were short and dark.'
segments.s665-g15: که introduces the descriptive clause. شهری خنک بود = 'a city was cool' (شَهری used adjectivally 'a city/urban place'), و روشن هوا = 'and the air was bright/clear' — adjectives follow the nouns; بود is the past copula 'was.' The whole paints the place as pleasantly cool with clear air.
segments.s665-g16: از آنجا گذشتن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to pass from that place' (infinitive used as the subject). نبودی is the negative past copula 'was not', and روا is the adjective 'permissible/fitting.' Word order is emphatic/poetic: 'to pass from there was not allowed/ proper.'
segments.s665-g137: This is a poetic prepositional phrase marking extent: ز + place + تا + در + place = 'from X to/in Y'. ز (‘from’) names the origin (Armenia) and تا ... در indicates the reach 'as far as/in Ardabil.' The order (تا در اردبیل) is idiomatic in older Persian poetry.
segments.s665-g138: پراگنده شد is a compound with the adjective/participle پراگنده ('scattered') and شد ('became' / auxiliary), yielding 'was scattered' or 'became scattered.' لشکرش is the subject with the possessive enclitic -ش ('his army'). The repeated خیل خیل is a poetic pluralizing/intensifying adverbial phrase meaning 'in hosts / in crowds,' following the noun it quantifies.
segments.s665-g139: Another origin phrase: ز دشت 'from the plain' followed by an apposition/list of the troops that came (سواران, نیزه‌گزار). Word order lists the types of fighters after the place of origin; there's no separate verb here because the verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s665-g140: سپاهی بیامد is a straightforward past-tense clause 'an army/soldiers came.' The phrase فزون از شمار is an idiom: فزون ('more/abundant') + از شمار ('than number') = 'more than can be counted' or 'beyond numbering.' Together: 'soldiers came in numbers beyond counting.'
segments.s665-g141: چو ... چو is a poetic way to introduce examples/figures: 'with Abbas and with Hamzeh' (literally 'as Abbas and as Hamzeh'). شان is the possessive 'their' referring back to the larger force, and پیشرو is a noun 'vanguard' (those in front). The whole chunk means 'with Abbas and Hamzeh as their vanguard.'
segments.s665-g142: This is a coordinated noun phrase: 'سواران و گردن‌فرازان نو' = 'horsemen and newly proud ones.' گردن‌فرازان ('neck-raisers') is a poetic epithet meaning 'proud/boastful warriors,' and نو ('new') qualifies them as recently risen or fresh troops.
segments.s665-g143: ز تاراج ('from/by plunder') gives the cause; ویران شد is the passive/intransitive result 'was laid waste/ruined.' آن بوم is the subject 'that land/homeland.' The coordinating و + رست links a subsequent contrasting/result clause: 'and it was delivered/saved.'
segments.s665-g144: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ('for/that'), giving the reason the land was saved. هرمز is treated here as a personal name; همی is an archaic/emphatic particle marking the action; باژ is a noun 'cry' or 'shout' and is linked to the verb بجست, which is a past tense verb 'leapt/sprung.' The clause reads 'that Hormuz indeed leapt at them with a cry' (i.e., he attacked them, which brought deliverance).
segments.s665-g225: سپهدارشان (their spahbed/commander) is a compound of سپهدار + -شان (their) and functions as the subject; پیش + خراد + بود is a locative/predicative sequence meaning 'stood before / in the presence of Khurad.' بود is simple past copula 'was/stood.' Word order is subject — locative phrase — copula.
segments.s665-g226: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('who/which'); با governs the nouns that follow ('with/possessed of'); فر و اورنگ are coordinated objects ('royal glory and throne'); the second با داد repeats the 'with' to link داد ('justice') into the same predicate; بود is the past copula linking these attributes to the antecedent. The clause describes qualities Khurad has.
segments.s665-g227: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came'; به + ارمینیه indicates direction/arrival 'into/to Armenia'; در سپاه here means 'with (his) army' or 'in the host' (a locative/instrumental phrase). Word order places the temporal clause first, then the directional/locative complement.
segments.s665-g228: سپاه خزر is a noun phrase 'the Khazar army' (modifier after head: army + Khazar); برگرفتند is a past transitive verb 'they took up / set out on' and here governs راه ('the road/path') as its object, yielding 'the Khazar army took the road.' The verb is in the simple past, 3rd person plural.
segments.s665-g229: وز is an archaic/prepositional form (here functioning like از 'from' or a connective), followed by ایشان 'them/they' as the object/target; فراوان modifies the verb as an adverb 'in great number'; بکشتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they killed'; نیز 'also' links this action to the following clause. The clause order is connective — object — manner — verb — linker.
segments.s665-g230: گرفتند is the 3rd person plural past 'they took/seized'; زان is a contracted form of 'از آن' meaning 'from that/there'; مرز is the locative object 'border/frontier'; بسیار چیز is a postpositive quantifier phrase 'many things' (بسیار modifies چیز). The verb + prepositional phrase pattern yields 'they seized many things from that border.'
segments.s665-g231: چو + آمد forms a temporal clause 'when ... came'; آگاهی is 'news/information' and is the grammatical subject of آمد; به نزدیک شاه is a locative/dative phrase 'to (near) the king' indicating the recipient. The structure is 'When information came to the king', i.e., 'when the king learned.'
segments.s665-g232: که introduces the reported content 'that ...'; خراد is the subject (here used as a designation 'the wise/Khurad'); پیروز is a predicative adjective 'victorious' and شد is the past copula/auxiliary 'became/was' giving 'became victorious'; با سپاه is an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with (his) army.' The clause reports the fact received by the king.
segments.s665-g153: برآورد is a past, 3rd-person singular perfective verb (poetic/classical word order puts the verb early) meaning 'brought forth' or 'revealed'. رازی is the direct object 'a secret'. که بود از نهفت is a relative clause modifying رازی: که = 'that', بود = past copula 'was', and از نهفت = 'from concealment / hidden' (نهفت = 'hidden'). Together: 'he brought forth a secret that was hidden.'
segments.s665-g154: بدان here functions as ب + (آن) 'to those' (classical contracted prepositional phrase) marking the indirect object. نامداران ایران = 'the renowned/illustrious ones of Iran' (نامداران modified by the genitive Iran). بگفت is past, 3rd-person singular 'he said / told (them)'. The clause as a whole: 'he told that to the renowned men of Iran.'
segments.s665-g155: که introduces a reported-content clause 'that...'. چندین = 'several / many a' modifying سپه 'host(s), army(ies)'. روی به ایران نهاد is a verbal phrase: روی here indicates direction/turning (face/toward) and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put / set', so 'set/marshaled many armies toward Iran.' Word order places the verb نهاد at the end (classical syntax).
segments.s665-g156: کسی in negative contexts here means 'no one'. در جهان = 'in the world'. این refers back to the foregoing matter ('this'). ندارد بیاد is a periphrastic negative expression: ندارد 'does not have' + بیاد (lit. 'in memory') = 'does not have [it] in memory' → 'does not remember'; so 'no one in the world remembers this.'
segments.s665-g157: همه نامداران = 'all the renowned (men)'. فرو ماندند is a compound verbal phrase: فرو (an adverbial intensifier 'down / deeply / utterly') + ماندند (past, 3rd-plural of ماندن 'to remain / be left'), here idiomatically 'were left stunned/abashed' or 'fell silent'. The verb is past plural agreeing with نامداران.
segments.s665-g158: ز هرگونه اندیشه‌ها: ز (poetic preposition 'from / because of') + هرگونه 'every kind of' + اندیشه‌ها 'thoughts'. راندند is past, 3rd-plural of راندن 'to drive (away)'; here it means 'were driven away from all sorts of thought' (they were driven away from thinking, i.e. bewildered). Note the object/oblique phrase precedes the verb in classical style.
segments.s665-g159: بگفتند is past, 3rd-person plural 'they said' (narrative reporting). کای شاه is a vocative address: کای 'O' (classical), شاه 'king'. با رای و هوش = 'with counsel (رای) and intellect/wisdom (هوش)' describing the qualities with which they beseech him. The whole preface: 'They said, "O king, with counsel and wisdom..."'.
segments.s665-g160: یکی here is an indefinite 'one / a certain' used like 'someone' or 'now' as a softener; اندرین کار = 'in this affair / to this matter' (اندرین = 'in/among these', کار = 'matter/affair'). بگشای is an imperative (2nd-person singular) 'open / give (it) up' in classical form; with گوش 'ear' the idiom بگشای گوش = 'open your ear' = 'lend an ear / listen'. So: 'Lend an ear to this matter.'
segments.s665-g161: Sequence of nominal phrases: 'خردمند شاهی' is a poetic vocative/address meaning “O king who is wise” (adjective before or around the title is normal in epic Persian). The conjunction 'و' links that address to 'ما کهتریم' where 'ما' is the 1st-person plural subject and 'کهتریم' is a present-state adjective/verb meaning “we are inferior.” Word order is emphatic: the praised addressee first, then the speaker’s self-positioning.
segments.s665-g162: 'همی' is an archaic/epic particle used here for emphasis before the verb; it intensifies or gives a hortatory feel. 'خویشتن' is emphatic 'ourselves'; 'موبدی' is a noun 'mobed' (Zoroastrian priest) used as the category being claimed; 'نشمریم' is 1st‑person plural negative form of شمر/شمردن (to count/consider) meaning 'let us not consider/count (ourselves as).’ Together the phrase issues a self‑restraining counsel: 'let us not consider ourselves mobeds.'
segments.s665-g163: 'براندیش' is an imperative/subjunctive form (addressing someone — 'consider/think over'). 'تا' here introduces the content/purpose clause ('so that' / 'in order to see what...') rather than literal 'until.' 'چاره کار چیست' is a nominal interrogative 'what is the remedy/solution of the affair' with 'چیست' (چی + ست) functioning as copula + interrogative.
segments.s665-g164: This is an interrogative nominal clause: 'بر و بوم' is a fused idiom meaning 'land and home/territory' (literal parts 'بر' + 'بوم'); 'ما را' marks possession/affectedness with the object marker 'را' (our land as definite object). 'نگهدار' is a noun here ('guardian, preserver') and 'کی ست' asks 'who is (it)?' — 'Who is the guardian of our land?'.
segments.s665-g165: 'چنین گفت موبد' is a fixed reporting formula: 'thus said the mobed.' Here 'چنین' = 'thus/so' and 'گفت' is used as the speech verb (in the source senses list it appears as the speech/utterance sense). 'که بودش وزیر' is a subordinate/explanatory clause introduced by 'که' = 'that/who': 'that who was/is to be his minister/vizier.' 'بودش' is the enclitic past/present copular form 'was/is (to him)' and 'وزیر' names the office.
segments.s665-g166: This is a direct address following the mobed's words: 'که‌ای' here is a vocative particle (roughly 'O' / 'who is it — O ...'). 'شاه' is the addressee 'king.' 'دانا' and 'دانش‌پذیر' are two coordinate adjectives: 'wise' and 'receptive to learning/teachable.' The phrase addresses the king as both wise and willing to learn.
segments.s665-g167: 'سپاه' is the subject 'host/army.' 'خزر‌گر' is an epithet attached to سپاه (here an ethnic/characterizing label — in context 'Khazar/Khazar‑like host' or a fur‑clad host; the lexeme list gives the occupational/descriptor sense). 'بیاید' is a present/subjunctive possibility 'should come' / 'may come.' 'به جنگ' is the directional/complement phrase 'to battle.' Together the line sets up a protasis: 'If a (Khazar) host comes to battle...'.
segments.s665-g168: 'نیابند' is a negative jussive/optative/imperative 3rd‑person form (negative of یابیدن) used here as a prohibition or counsel in generalizing sense: 'let them not find / do not allow them to find / do not seek.' 'جنگی' functions as an object/theme 'a battle/fight.' 'زمانی' = 'for a time' or 'even a moment' and 'درنگ' = 'delay, pause.' The phrase as a whole warns: 'do not seek battle; do not delay even a moment.'
segments.s665-g41: This is a verbal past-clause with the finite verb placed first (poetic inversion): بیامد (came) + subject خداوند (the lord/owner) + locator آن کشتزار (that field). In classical Persian poetry the verb often opens the clause; here it sets the scene ('came the lord of that field').
segments.s665-g42: به پیش موکل is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in front of / before the keeper' (به پیش = toward/in the presence of + موکل = the entrusted guardian). بنالید is the past tense 'moaned/wailed' and زار functions adverbially ('piteously'/'in a wretched manner'). The clause follows the opening verb-clause and gives the action performed before the keeper.
segments.s665-g43: موکل بدو گفت is a simple past reporting clause: subject موکل (the keeper), indirect object بدو ('to him'), and گفت ('said' — the past verb). The following phrase کین اسب کیست is an embedded interrogative/nominal clause: کین here is a demonstrative/identifying word (see senses note), اسب 'horse', and کیست is the archaic fused copula 'کی + ست' meaning 'who/whose is it' or 'what is it'.
segments.s665-g44: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which' giving the reason. بر دم و گوشش is a prepositional phrase with بر 'upon/on' and two coordinated nouns دم and گوش with the enclitic possessive ش 'its'. Note: دم here refers to the animal's tail (classical usage). بباید is a modal present 'it is necessary/one must' and is followed by گریست (an older/poetic infinitive-like form 'to weep' / 'weep'), so بباید گریست = 'one must weep'.
segments.s665-g45: خداوند گفت begins with the speaker 'the lord/master said' (گفت is past-tense 'said'). اسب پرویز شاه is a noun phrase where the title/name follows the noun (poetic omission of ezāfe): 'the horse of Parviz the king' (i.e. King Parviz's steed). Such postpositive epithets are common in classical Persian.
segments.s665-g46: ندارد is present-tense 'does not have' / 'lacks'. همی is a petty poetic particle giving emphasis ('indeed/at all' / continuous sense). کهتران را is the definite/direct-object phrase 'the lesser/lowly ones' marked by را, and نگاه is the object noun 'regard/glance'. Word order places the verb before the emphasized particle and object (typical poetic ordering): 'has no regard for the lowly.'
segments.s665-g47: Another verb-first clause: بیامد (came) + موکل (the keeper/entrusted one) + بر شهریار (to/toward the sovereign). بر here means 'toward/to' and شهریار is the addressee 'the king/ruler'. This is a simple motion clause with poetic word order (verb before subject).
segments.s665-g48: بگفت is past 'he told/said' followed by the content clause introduced by آنچ 'that which / what' and the verb بشنید 'he heard' with source phrase از کشتزار 'from the field'. The whole segment is a reported-speech object: 'he told (to the ruler) that which he had heard from the field.'
segments.s665-g217: چون ... رسید is a temporal clause: چون here means 'when/since' and + past verb رسید gives 'when he reached/arrived.' پیش followed by a proper name means 'in the presence of / before' (i.e. at Caesar's court). The whole segment is a simple past narrative clause: 'the messenger, when he reached before Caesar.'
segments.s665-g218: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said / told.' آنچ is the poetic contraction of آن‌چه 'that which / what.' از introduces the source 'from' (i.e. what he heard from someone). شنید is simple past 'heard.' Together: 'he said what he had heard from the King of Iran.'
segments.s665-g219: ز ره بازگشت is an ablative + past-verb phrase: ز (poetic 'from') + ره 'road/route' + بازگشت 'returned' = 'returned from the road.' آن زمان is the adverbial 'at that time' and شاه روم is the subject 'the king of Rome' placed after the time phrase, a poetic inversion of normal subject–verb order.
segments.s665-g220: نیاورد is the negated past 'did not bring/produce.' جنگ is the direct object 'war.' اندر(ان) here supplies a locative sense 'therein/within,' and مرز و بوم is a fixed pairing 'border and homeland' (i.e. their territory). So the clause states that he did not bring war into those borders/lands.
segments.s665-g221: سپاهی از ایرانیان برگزید is 'he chose/selected an army from the Iranians.' سپاهی functions as a collective noun 'an army/soldiers,' از introduces the source 'from among,' and برگزید is the past transitive 'selected.' The subject (he) is omitted as usual in Persian narrative.
segments.s665-g222: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'سپاهی.' از گردشان = 'from around them' (گرد here means 'around/about'); روز شد ناپدید is a poetic word order meaning 'became invisible/vanished by day' (روز 'day' + شد 'became' + ناپدید 'vanished'). The clause describes that they disappeared from view even in daylight.
segments.s665-g223: فرستادشان is the past transitive 'he sent them.' تا here introduces purpose ('so that / in order that') and governs the following subjunctive clause. بران بوم و بر is a poetic phrase meaning 'to/onto that land and region' (بران 'that/thereon' + بوم و بر 'land and region').
segments.s665-g224: به پای اندر آرند is a purpose/result clause: به پای 'to the foot (of)' + اندر 'into/within' + آرند (archaic/subjunctive/plural form) meaning 'they should enter / set (their) foot.' This construction means 'so that they would set foot within...' مرز خزر specifies the place 'the Khazar frontier/border.'
segments.s665-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. پنهان is an adjectival/past-participial form 'hidden', and شدی is the past-tense verb 'became / was' (classical past 3rd sg), so چو پنهان شدی = 'when (it) became hidden'. چادر لاژورد is a noun phrase 'lapis (blue) tent/curtain' placed after the clause; the color-word لاژورد modifies چادر.
segments.s665-g18: پدید آمدی is a classical/poetic past verb phrase 'appeared / came into view' (پدید + آمدی). The following words کوه یاقوت زرد form the noun phrase 'a mountain of ruby (ruby mountain) that is yellow' which functions as the subject or emergent thing of پدید آمدی; Persian allows the verb to precede the noun phrase here.
segments.s665-g19: منادیگری is a deverbal/nominal form meaning 'heralding' or 'as a herald' and here sets the manner of the action. برکشیدی is the past 2nd-person singular 'you raised up' (بر + کشیدن), and خروش is the direct object 'a cry / shout' — together 'you raised a heralding cry.'
segments.s665-g20: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory address (classical ordering of که + ای) introducing the words spoken or called out. نامداران is the vocative 'renowned/illustrious ones', and با فر و هوش is a prepositional phrase 'with farr (royal glory) and sense/awareness' qualifying those addressed.
segments.s665-g21: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کشتمندی (کشتمند) is a noun/adjective meaning 'slayer' or 'deadly one' serving as the subject of the clause. شود is the classical present/subjunctive/past auxiliary 'becomes / should become', and کوفته is a past-participial adjective 'crushed / struck down' predicated of کشتمندی: 'if the slayer is/should be crushed.'
segments.s665-g22: وزان is a fused prepositional phrase 'from that / because of that' (referring back to the crushing). رنج کارنده is a noun phrase 'a piercing/biting sorrow' (رنج 'pain/suffering' qualified by کارنده 'piercing'). آشوفته is a predicative past-participle/adjective 'disturbed / disordered / confounded', so the clause says that from such piercing suffering he becomes distraught.
segments.s665-g23: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if'. اسب is the subject 'horse'. در + کشتزاری is the locative phrase 'into / in a cultivated field' (کشتزاری = farmland, cultivated land). رود here is a verbal form 'goes' (classical 3rd sg subjunctive/ present), so the clause reads 'if a horse goes into a cultivated field.' Note: the lemma senses list supplied for رود gave noun senses; here رود functions as a verb.
segments.s665-g24: کسی نیز is 'someone also / someone likewise' (کسی = someone, نیز = also). بر + میوه‌داری is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to / upon the fruit-place' i.e. 'to the orchard / fruit-garden' (میوه‌داری = fruit-bearing place). رود again is the verbal 'goes'. The pattern mirrors the previous clause to express a parallel consequence: if the horse goes into the field, someone likewise goes into the orchard.
segments.s665-g321: This noun phrase is arranged with the epithet before the verb: جهان‌جوی (subject) + چوبینه (epithet/label) + دارد (verb) + لقب (object). دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/possesses' used here in narrative description. چوبینه is an attributive noun functioning as the title he 'has' (i.e. ‘the epithet “Chubineh”’).
segments.s665-g322: This clause states origin of descent with an inverted order: هم (‘also’) + از (‘from’) + پهلوانانش (‘his champions’) + باشد (copula) + نسب (‘lineage’). باشد here functions as a present/subjunctive copula used in narrative to link نسب to its source; نسب appears at the end for emphasis (‘his lineage is from the champions’).
segments.s665-g323: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. This noun phrase is appositional: این مرد (this man) followed by two appositives describing him—چاکر (retainer) and باندک سپاه (of a small troop). The modifiers follow the head, typical in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s665-g324: ز جایی بیاید is a prepositional + verb phrase meaning 'came from some place'; here بیاید is a narrative-form verb ('came') used where modern Persian would use آمد. به درگاه شاه is the directional phrase 'to the king’s court/presence' with درگاه as 'royal court/threshold' and شاه as the possessor.
segments.s665-g325: مرین ترک را is the definite object ('Marin the Turk' with را marking the object). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'. بشکند is a narrative-form verb (3rd person) meaning 'he smote/overthrew/ broke' — a poetic past-tense usage of the verb 'to break/defeat' applied to a person/object.
segments.s665-g326: همه لشکرش را is the object phrase 'his whole army' (همه intensifies لشکرش) with را. بهم is an adverb meaning 'into disorder / scattered', and برزند is a plural narrative-form verb meaning 'they scatter' or 'are scattered' in context; together the phrase means 'they routed/scattered his entire host.' (The final verb is an archaic/narrative form used for past action.)
segments.s665-g327: چو بشنید is a temporal clause 'when he heard'. گفت is the narrative past 'said' introducing direct speech, and the following اسم (ستاره شمر) is the speaker identification: 'said Shamr the Star' — an inverted order common in epic where the verb precedes the name of the speaker, and ستاره functions as an epithet attached to شمر.
segments.s665-g328: ندیدم is first-person past negative 'I have not seen'. ز here functions as the comparative 'than' ('from the khaghan'), خاقان is the reference point 'the khaghan', کسی is the indefinite 'anyone', and شادتر is the comparative adjective 'happier' at the end of the clause — together: 'I have not seen anyone happier than the khaghan.'
segments.s665-g169: ابا here is a noun/adverb used hortatively — literally 'reluctance' used with the sense 'without reluctance' or 'let us not be reluctant'. The phrase رومیان داستان‌ها زدن is a poetic idiom 'to sing/tell stories about the Romans': رومیان ('the Romans') is the topical object, داستان‌ها is the direct object 'tales/stories', and the verb زنیم (1pl) appears at the end as a hortative 'let us sing/tell'.
segments.s665-g170: ز introduces the source/partitive 'from'; بن پایه is a tautological poetic pair meaning 'root and foundation' (intensifying 'foundation'); تازیان 'the Arabs' is the genitive/possessive target of those foundations; برکنیم (1pl subjunctive/optative) is a hortative 'let us uproot/remove/throw off'. Word order places the objects before the verb, typical in classical Persian verse.
segments.s665-g171: ندارم is 1sg present negative 'I do not have' (the subject 'I' is incorporated in the verb); به دل is a prepositional phrase 'in (my) heart' placed before بیم for emphasis; از تازیان 'of/from the Arabs' marks the source of the fear. Overall: 'I have no fear in my heart of the Arabs.'
segments.s665-g172: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); از دیدشان 'from their sight' means 'from being seen by them'; دیده is a past-participial/nominalized form 'being seen' (or 'the act of seeing'), and دارد زیان literally 'has harm' i.e. 'brings/causes harm'. Together: 'for being seen by them brings harm.'
segments.s665-g173: که begins an explanatory clause ('for'/'that'); the correlative هم ... و هم ... = 'both ... and ...' links two descriptions. مارخوارند is a descriptive verb phrase 'they eat snakes' (literally 'are snake‑eaters') used figuratively; سوسمار is a further disparaging image 'lizard' (here a pejorative/derogatory attribute).
segments.s665-g174: ندارند is 3pl negative 'they do not have'; جنگی modifies کارزار (a warlike/battle) and گه 'occasion/time' qualifies when — 'at the time of battle'. The phrase means they lack a proper battlefield/war‑capacity: 'they have no (real) battlefield in war‑time.' Word order places the negated verb before the noun phrase in this poetic construction.
segments.s665-g175: تو را marks the dative/experiencer 'to you' (را here emphasizes the indirect object); ساوه is the place/name Saveh; شاهست is the fused archaic form of شاه است 'is (a) king' (here 'the king of Saveh' is meant by the phrase); نزدیک‌تر is the comparative 'nearer' placed after the copula for emphasis: 'To you the king of Saveh is nearer.'
segments.s665-g176: وزو is a compressed poetic sequence (read as و ز او = 'and from him / because of him'); کار ما 'our affairs/work' is the possessed noun phrase; نیز 'also' links the clause; تاریک‌تر is comparative 'darker/more obscure'. The clause thus says that because of him, their affairs are even darker. (Note: the printed lemma 'وزو' as 'ablution' is not the sense here — this is a poetic contraction of و ز او.)
segments.s665-g25: دم و گوش اسبش is a coordinated noun phrase meaning “tail and ears of his horse” (Persian often omits the ezāfe here). بباید + برید expresses obligation: بباید (must/it is necessary) precedes the main verb برید (here jussive/past-stem used poetically to mean “cut off”). Word order places the object phrase before the modal + verb at the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s665-g26: سر دزد is the noun phrase “the thief's head” (head + thief); بر دار is a prepositional phrase meaning “on the gallows” (بر = on/upon, دار = gallows). باید کشید is the obligation construction with باید (must) followed by کشید — here کشید is used in the sense “to hoist/display/drag” (poetic use) and sits at the sentence end. The modal precedes the lexical verb, as expected.
segments.s665-g27: بدو is a locative/dative adverb meaning “to him/unto him.” ماه گردان is a poetic compound: ماه (moon) + گردان (revolving/turning) functioning as an attributive phrase modifying بدی (evil/wrongdoing). در جهان is the locative “in the world,” placed after the noun phrase for emphasis; classical word order and poetic compounds can separate modifiers and noun.
segments.s665-g28: بد و نیکویی is the paired nouns “bad (evil) and goodness,” i.e. “both evil and good.” زو is the enclitic contraction of ز + او meaning “from him.” نبودی is the negative past form of بودن (was not) and here negates نهان (“hidden”); the word order is inverted for poetic emphasis: literally “evil and goodness from him was not hidden,” i.e. “nothing good or bad was hidden from him.”
segments.s665-g29: بهر کشوری is a prepositional phrase “for the country/for the realm” (بهر = for/toward + کشوری “of the country/realm”). داد کردی is a light-noun + verb construction: داد (justice) as the object/noun and کردی (you did) as the verb, together meaning “you administered justice.” چنین (“thus”) is an adverb modifying the whole action and appears at the end for emphasis.
segments.s665-g30: ز دهقان is “from the peasant” (ز = from, دهقان = peasant). همی‌یافتی is the archaic/progressive form formed with همی + past-root (یافتی) and here means “you received/you would receive” (past/habitual sense). آفرین is a noun “praise/acclamation” serving as the object: “you received praise from the peasant.” Note the older prefix همی that gives a continuous/habitual shade.
segments.s665-g31: پسر بد is the noun phrase “a (lit. ‘bad’) son” — in classical contexts adjectival modifiers can have nuanced senses but grammatically it is the head noun plus modifier. مر او را is a dative + object cluster: مر (me) + او را (him) meaning “to me him” → “he made him dear to me” or “he prized him for me.” گرامی یکی is an appositive phrase “an honored one, a certain one” (گرامی = dear/honored, یکی = one/a certain), placed after the object for emphasis.
segments.s665-g32: که is the relative pronoun “who/that.” از ماه introduces a comparison “than the moon” (از used poetically for comparison). پیدا نبود اندکی is an inverted negative predicate: پیدا نبود (was not apparent/was not lacking) + اندکی (a little) — idiomatically this means “was not a little less than the moon,” i.e. “he was hardly/only slightly inferior to the moon in beauty.” The negative + quantity word gives a comparative sense.
segments.s665-g1: بدانست is a past-tense verb 'understood/knew' placed before its subject (hermez) in the elevated/poetic word order. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that ...'). او is the third-person pronoun 'he', and دست خون is a compact noun phrase ('hand [of] blood' = 'bloody hand' or 'to shed blood') functioning as the core idea of the subordinate clause.
segments.s665-g2: بیازد is a poetic/archaic verb here meaning 'break forth / let fall' (i.e. 'shed'); همی is the old verbal particle that gives continuous or emphatic aspect (here: 'would/ever'); زنده is a predicative/adverbial 'alive'; بی‌رهنمون is an adjective phrase 'without guidance' modifying the subject or manner of the action. Together they complete the subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s665-g3: شنید is simple past 'heard' and is followed by its object phrase; آن specifies 'those/the', سخن‌های is the plural noun 'words/speeches' and بی‌کام qualifies them as 'fruitless/unfulfilled'. The postposed را is the definite-object marker marking the whole NP as the direct object of شنید.
segments.s665-g4: به زندان is a prepositional phrase of destination ('to/into prison'). فرستاد is the past transitive verb 'sent'; بهرام را is the definite direct object (name + را). Note the verb appears before the object in this poetic line but the object is still marked by را.
segments.s665-g5: دگر شب = 'another/one more night' (دگر = 'another'). چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; برزد is an archaic past verb 'rose/appeared' with سر ('head') as its subject, and از کوه ماه is a genitive-like phrase 'from the mountain of the moon' (i.e. 'as the moon rose above the mountain'). The clause is a temporal circumstance for the main action.
segments.s665-g6: به زندان is again a locative/directional phrase 'to/into the prison'; دژآگاه is a compound noun 'fortress-warden/prison-guard' that qualifies the prison or names its keeper; کردش is the past tense of کردن with the enclitic object -ش 'him' (i.e. 'made him / caused him to be'); تباه is a resultative adjective 'ruined'. Grammatically، 'کردش تباه' = 'made him ruined', with the preceding prepositional phrase giving location/agent nuance.
segments.s665-g7: نماند is past negative 'did not remain/there remained not'. آن زمان‌بر is a nominal phrase 'that time-consuming / time-worn (prudence/way)' functioning as the patient that did not remain. درش is در + enclitic ش 'in his/at his door/house' (در here = 'door/house'); بخردی is a noun 'wisdom/prudence' completing the thought. The line puts the negated verb first (poetic inversion) and then the displaced NP with possessive enclitic.
segments.s665-g8: همان is emphatic 'that very / the same'; رهنمائی means 'guidance'; و هم موبدی lists an additional item 'and even the mobed/priest' (هم adding the 'even/also' sense). Together these nouns specify what exactly did not remain in his house.
segments.s665-g241: به کنجی is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb: به (towards/into) + کنجی (a nook/corner) = “in a corner.” نشستست is an archaic fused form (نشست + است) meaning ‘has sat’ or ‘is seated’ (3rd person). The clause continues with با زند و است, where با + زند (literally ‘with life/living’) plus the copula است yields the sense ‘and he lives / and is alive’ — a poetic way to assert his living state.
segments.s665-g242: ز امید گیتی is an ablative/causal phrase: ز (from/because of) + امید (hope) + گیتی (the world) = ‘from/because of hope in the world.’ شده is the passive/perfect participle ‘has become,’ and پیر و سست are predicative adjectives joined by و (and): ‘old and weak/feeble.’ The whole phrase means he has become old and frail due to worldly hopes.
segments.s665-g243: بدین روزگاران is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in these times’ (بدین = ‘in/with these’ in archaic/poetic usage) followed by روزگاران ‘the days/ages.’ بر او شدم literally puts بر (upon/toward) + او (him) before the verb شدم (I became/happened) — here an idiomatic, older way to say ‘I went to him / I came upon him.’
segments.s665-g244: یکی روز و یک شب = ‘one day and one night.’ The phrase بر او again marks the person with whom the time was spent (‘with him / at his place’), and the final word بدم is an archaic verbal form meaning here ‘I spent / I gave (time)’ — the whole clause expresses that the speaker stayed with that man for a day and a night.
segments.s665-g245: همی‌گفتم is the iterative/continuous past (همی + گفتم) meaning ‘I was saying’ or ‘I kept telling.’ او را uses را to mark the person addressed/affected (‘to him’ in older usage). من is emphatic ‘I.’ از introduces the topic/content ‘about’ or ‘of,’ so از ساوه شاه conveys ‘about the king of Saveh’ (modern order would be شاهِ ساوه).
segments.s665-g246: ز here functions like ‘of’/‘concerning’ to introduce the description: پیلان (plural of پیل) ‘elephants’ modified by جنگی (‘warlike’ or ‘military’), so پیلان جنگی = ‘war-elephants’ or ‘elephants used in war.’ و چندان سپاه = ‘and so many an army’ (چندان = ‘so many / such a great’ modifying سپاه ‘army’). The adjective/order reflect poetic inversion.
segments.s665-g247: چنین داد پاسخ = ‘he gave such an answer’ (چنین = thus/such, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer) — straightforward verb-object order. چو آمد سخن is a temporal subordinate clause: چو = when, آمد = came, سخن = word/tale — ‘when the tale came’ (i.e., when the story was told).
segments.s665-g248: ازان گفته = ‘from that said/account’ (ازان = from that, گفته = said/account) — a genitive/source phrase pointing to an earlier tale. روزگار کهن = ‘ancient time(s)’ (روزگار = time/fate, کهن = old/ancient), giving the provenance of the story.
segments.s665-g49: بدو = be + do, a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the 3rd-person past of گفتن ('said') though the provided sense list lacks that sense; هرمز is the speaker (proper name) following the verb by poetic inversion. برفتن is the verbal-nominal infinitive formed with the prefix بر- + رفتن 'to go/leave', and بکوش is the present-imperative/subjunctive 'try/endeavor' (ب- prefix + کوش). Together برفتن بکوش = 'try to depart'.
segments.s665-g50: ببر is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'take!' with اسب را as the definite direct object (را marking the object after the noun). در زمان + noun phrase gives a temporal adjunct 'in/at the time of ...'; دم و گوش is an idiomatic short phrase literally 'breath and ear' used adverbially for a very brief instant or while attention (گوش) is engaged/distracted. Word order places the prepositional phrase after the command.
segments.s665-g51: زیانی is a noun 'loss/damage' in the nominative position for the clause; که introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. آمد here means 'befell/occurred' (came to), and بران is the archaic/poetic prepositional adverb 'upon them/thereon'. کشتمند in this context refers to the slain/killed (a poetic participial/compound), but this lexicon entry does not supply an exact match, so its listed senses do not capture the use as 'the slain'.
segments.s665-g52: شمارش is the noun 'counting/reckoning' and functions as the thing required; بباید is an impersonal modal 'it must / one must' (archaic b- prefix plus باید). شمردن is the infinitive 'to count', and که چند 'how many' specifies the content of the counting. The sequence 'شمارش بباید شمردن که چند' is a poetic, slightly tautological way of saying 'the counting must be: to count how many.'
segments.s665-g53: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. خسرو here is the source (the name Khosrow/the king). زیان is 'loss/damage'. باز before باید has the sense 'back / again' implying restitution, and باید ستد is an impersonal construction 'it must be exacted/recovered' (باید 'must' + ستد, classical verb meaning 'to take/exact'). Word order places the prepositional phrase first for emphasis and uses the impersonal modal construction for obligation.
segments.s665-g54: اگر ... اگر is a repeated conditional 'if ... if ...' used idiomatically to list amounts without changing the obligation. زیانست is a fused copular/adjectival form meaning 'it is loss' or 'it counts as loss' — here indicating 'even if it is a loss of a hundred'. The omitted numerals in the printed line supply the quantities ('صد' = one hundred, 'پانصد' = five hundred).
segments.s665-g55: درم‌های is the plural of درم 'dirham/coin' with the plural suffix; گنجی modifies it as 'treasure-related' ('treasury dirhams' or 'treasure coins'). بران is the poetic adverbial 'upon that' and کشتزار is the noun 'planted field/cropland' receiving the action. The noun phrase places the money and its qualifier together before the locative adverb.
segments.s665-g56: بریزند is 3rd-person plural present-subjunctive/jussive of ریختن used to express a command or instruction ('let them pour' / 'they should pour'). پیش is the preposition 'before / in the presence of', خداوند here means 'the lord/sovereign' (contextually likely the ruler), and کار is 'affair/matter' — together 'present the matter before the lord'. The verb governs both the pouring action (direct object implicit) and the presentation of the affair.
segments.s665-g113: This is a from–to phrase: ز ... تا ... gives the span ‘from X up to Y.’ در here is the noun dar, ‘gate/door,’ not the preposition; word order is poetic but literal: 'from the plain of Hari as far as the gate of Marv‑rud.'
segments.s665-g114: سپه (subject) + بود (past copula) + آگنده (predicate adjective) — 'the army was full/filled.' چون introduces a simile comparing that fullness to تار و پود (warp and weft), a fixed woven‑cloth image meaning tightly filled or interwoven.
segments.s665-g115: An adverbial/nominal phrase + verb: وزین‌روی is a descriptive phrase 'with a dignified/grave face,' تا مرو marks the destination 'as far as Merv,' and لشکر کشید is the past action 'the army moved/marched/drew on.' کشید is the past of کشیدن ('to draw/march'), with لشکر as the subject placed before the verb in classical word order.
segments.s665-g116: شد is the past/copular verb 'became/was,' followed by the ablative phrase از گرد لشکر 'from the dust of the army' and the subject/predicate زمین ناپدید 'the ground (became) invisible/vanished.' The clause order is a bit inverted (شد comes early) but reads: 'from the army's dust the ground became unseen.'
segments.s665-g117: Typical classical SOV order: subject بهر مز, object یکی نامه ('a certain letter' / 'a letter'), and the past verb بنوشت 'wrote.' شاه follows as the indirect object ('to the king') with no preposition, a common classical usage.
segments.s665-g118: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). نزدیک خود خوان is an instructive/jussive phrase meaning 'call/summon near to himself' — خوان here is an archaic/jussive form 'call' (not the nominal 'banquet'), and ز هر سو سپاه is an ablative phrase 'armies from every side.' The clause gives the king's summons to gather troops from all directions.
segments.s665-g119: Two imperatives in sequence: برو 'go' followed by راه این لشکر آباد کن — 'make the way/route of this army prosperous.' راه is the object of آباد کن ('make prosperous'); آباد + کن is the causative/imperative pattern 'make X prosperous/inhabited.'
segments.s665-g120: A pair of coordinated imperatives joined by و: علف ساز — here ساز functions as an imperative 'make/prepare' (i.e. 'provide grass/fodder'), and از تیغ ما یاد کن is another imperative 'and mention/remember our sword.' یاد کن is the common imperative 'remember/mention.'
segments.s665-g185: عرض را بخوان — a direct imperative clause: عرض (the petition/report presented to the king) is the definite object marked by را; بخوان is the imperative of خواندن 'to read/recite'. Word order is object + را + verb (typical in Persian when the object is topicalized).
segments.s665-g186: تا بیارد شمار که چندست مردم که آید به کار — تا introduces a purposive / result clause 'so that / in order that'; بیارد is a 3rd-person form meaning 'he may bring' (purpose: bring the tally). شمار is the thing brought 'the count/tally'. که introduces the content of that tally: چندست 'how many' + مردم 'people'; the second که introduces the subordinate verb آید 'will come' (3rd-person present/subjunctive sense), followed by به کار 'to the work/for the business' as the goal. The clause is a nested subordinate content clause (that the tally will show ...).
segments.s665-g187: عرض با جریده به نزدیک شاه — a noun phrase with two accompanying prepositional phrases. عرض 'the petition/report' is the head; با + جریده 'with a writ/record' indicates accompaniment or that the petition was presented with a written paper; به نزدیک شاه 'toward/near the king' marks the direction/goal of motion. Near/goal phrase uses the older construction 'به نزدیکِ شاه' (toward the king).
segments.s665-g188: بیامد بیاورد بی‌مر سپاه — بیامد is the main past-tense verb 'came' (3rd sg.). The following بیاورد functions as a purpose/infinitival idea 'to bring' (here 'came in order to bring' or 'came bringing'); classical Persian often strings verbs/clauses like this for purpose without an explicit conjunction. بی‌مر is an archaic/obscure modifier whose precise sense is uncertain in the manuscript; سپاه 'the army' is the object/semantic target of بیاورد (i.e., bring news of / bring the army).
segments.s665-g189: شمار سپاه آمدش صد هزار — شمار سپاه 'the army's count/tally' is the subject/topic; آمدش 'came to him / was reported to him' combines آمد + enclitic -ش (to him). Following it, صد هزار is the reported number (numeral placed after the verb/statement in this older narrative style).
segments.s665-g190: پیاده بسی در میان سوار — a verbless/nominal phrase describing composition: پیاده 'infantry / those on foot' is named first; بسی is an intensifier 'many / a great many' (classical usage; the provided lexeme senses do not list this common classical meaning, so it is treated as an intensifier here); در میان سوار 'among the horsemen' is a locative phrase indicating these foot soldiers are counted alongside mounted troops. Word order is descriptive rather than a full clause.
segments.s665-g191: بدو گفت موبد که با ساوه شاه — بدو 'to him' (dative clitic phrase) + گفت 'said' forms 'said to him'; موبد is the speaker 'the mōbed/priest'. The following که introduces indirect speech or advice. با ساوه here functions as a directional/goal phrase 'to Saveh' (classical narrative often uses با + place for movement toward a locality), and شاه is the vocative or address 'O king' (word order here places the place-reference before the vocative).
segments.s665-g192: سزد‌گر نشوریم با این سپاه — سزدگر is an impersonal/adjectival form meaning 'it is fitting/appropriate'; what follows is a hortatory/subjunctive construction. نشوریم is an archaic/poetic verb form (here used as a hortative/subjunctive: 'we should set out / we should march' or 'let us go out'); this root is older and can mean 'to set oneself in motion' or 'to rise/rouse' depending on context. با این سپاه 'with this army' is the instrumental/means phrase completing the advice. Because these are older poetic forms, sense and polarity (encouraging vs. discouraging) rely on context — here the context supports a recommendation to move with the army.
segments.s665-g89: تو is the emphatic 2nd‑person pronoun (you). با + شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'with the king'. آشنایی is a noun ('acquaintance/familiarity') used predicatively, and the negative imperative مکن ('do not') follows it — a typical classical Persian order meaning 'do not make acquaintance (with...)'.
segments.s665-g90: خریده is a past‑participle/nominal use meaning 'that which is bought' or 'the purchased (thing)'; نداری here functions as a noun meaning 'lack/that which lacks (worth)'; بهایی is the noun 'value/price' and مکن is again the negative imperative 'do not'. Together the clause says literally 'do not (assign) value (بهایی مکن) to a purchased thing that has lack (خریده نداری)'.
segments.s665-g91: سپاسی is the noun 'thanks/praise'. نهم is the 1st‑person classical verb (from نهادن/دادن) 'I give' (often used after a noun to mean 'I give X'); بر تو is the indirect object 'to you'; بر زین کمر is a prepositional phrase 'on/round the saddle at the waist' (بر + زین + کمر).
segments.s665-g92: بپیچی is a 2nd‑person jussive/imperative form of پیچیدن 'to wrap/gird/turn' meaning 'gird (it) on' or 'wrap (the girth)'; اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; بشنود is a 3rd‑person (subjunctive/optative) form of شنیدن 'he should hear' or 'he hears'; دادگر is an epithet 'the just one', here referring to a just ruler/judge. The clause therefore means 'gird (it) if the just one hears.'
segments.s665-g93: یکی is the indefinite 'one/a certain'; مرد is 'man' and here functions as the head noun 'a man' (in epic contexts often 'a hero'); بد is an adjective immediately following مرد — in classical usage it can be pejorative 'bad' but in heroic poetry may be part of a conventional epithet; هرمز شهریار places the personal name Hurmuz (هرمز) with the title شهریار 'the king' (apposition). The whole phrase introduces 'a certain man — Hormoz the king.'
segments.s665-g94: به + پیروزی is the prepositional phrase 'by/through victory'; اندر (classical preposition) = 'in/within' or linking particle; شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'has become' (from شدن); نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/famous'. Together they form 'became famed/renowned through victory.'
segments.s665-g95: بمردی is an adverbial/ablative use meaning 'in manliness' or 'for his manhood/valor' (به + مردی); ستوده is the past participle 'praised' (used predicatively); به هر انجمن is the prepositional phrase 'in every assembly' or 'in every gathering' — together: 'praised for his manhood in every gathering.'
segments.s665-g96: که is the relative/conjunction 'that/who'; از رزم = 'from battle' (از as a source/arena); هرگز 'never'; ندیدی is the 2nd‑person past 'you did not see' (classical word order places the negative before the verb); شکن is a nominal which here means 'one who is broken/defeated' (or 'breaker' in other contexts), so the clause says 'that from battle you never saw (him) broken/defeated.'
segments.s665-g201: This is a rhetorical interrogative: چه آمد = "what happened/befell" (چه 'what' + آمد 'came/happened'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' indicating source or cause, so ز تیمار = 'from care/affliction'. بر شهر بلخ = 'upon the city of Balkh', with the preposition بر placed before the noun phrase.
segments.s665-g202: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('that'), شد is the past of شدن = 'became', and زندگانی is the noun 'life' (subject of شد). بران is an archaic contraction of بر + آن = 'upon that', linking back to 'شهر بلخ' (= 'on that land'), and تلخ is used figuratively 'bitter/harsh'.
segments.s665-g203: چنین تا = 'thus until'. گشاده شد is a passive/ resultive phrase literally 'was opened' or 'became open'; in poetic usage it conveys becoming able/ready/expanded (i.e. coming of maturity or full capacity). اسفندیار is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb in this inverted, poetic order.
segments.s665-g204: همی‌بود is an archaic imperfect/past continuous form meaning 'kept being' or 'continued to be'. هر گونه کارزار is a noun phrase 'every/any kind of battle' (هر 'every' + گونه 'kind/type' + کارزار 'battle'). The whole clause states that all sorts of warfare continued during that time.
segments.s665-g205: ز مهتر بسال = a compact prepositional phrase 'by a year from the elder' (ز = 'from/of', مهتر = 'the elder', بسال = 'by a year' or 'in years'); ار چه functions here as the concessive 'although' (literally 'if that'), and من کهترم is the personal statement 'I am the younger/inferior'. Word order is poetic but the sense is concessive: 'Although I am younger by a year than the elder...'.
segments.s665-g206: ازو من باندیشه بر بگذرم = 'I, in thought, pass beyond him.' ازو = 'from him/of him' (marking the one surpassed), من = 'I' topicalized, باندیشه = 'with thought / in counsel', بر بگذرم is a present/subjunctive-form verb phrase 'I pass/exceed' (بگذرم here = 'exceed/surpass'). The clause expresses a contrast: though younger in years, the speaker outstrips the elder in intellect/counsel.
segments.s665-g207: به موبد = dative 'to the mobed' (priest). چنین گفت = 'said thus' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'). پس شهریار places the subject 'the king' after the verb in poetic inversion; so the literal order is 'to the mobed thus said then the king' meaning 'then the king said thus to the mobed.'
segments.s665-g208: که introduces the content clause (what was said). قیصر نجوید = 'the Caesar does not/will not seek' — نجوید is the present/jussive sense 'seek/desire' in this context; ز ما = 'from/us' meaning 'against us' or 'with respect to us', and کارزار = 'war/battle'. The line expresses the king's wish or report that the Caesar will not desire war with them.
segments.s665-g81: چرا is an interrogative/exclamatory particle meaning “why.” رنج‌نابرده is a compound adjective/nominative phrase (literally “not having borne toil”) describing the person — here ‘inexperienced / innocent of hardship.’ کردی is the simple past 2nd-person singular “you did.” تباه is a predicative adjective “ruined/corrupt” that completes the verbal idea (i.e. “you made [it] ruined” or “you are ruined”).
segments.s665-g82: بنالم is a 1st‑person verbal form with the poetic/preserved prefix ب‑ (here functioning as present/subjunctive/optative in narrative) meaning “I lament” / “I will lament.” کنون is the adverb “now.” از تو is a prepositional phrase “because of you / against you.” در پیش شاه is a locative prepositional phrase “in the presence of the king / before the king.” Together: “Now I lament because of you before the king.”
segments.s665-g83: This is a noun phrase + causal phrase: سوار دلاور = “the brave rider/horseman” (noun + epithet). ز (poetic variant of از) + بیم زیان means “out of fear of loss” / “for fear of losing [something]”; ز links the rider to the motive for his action.
segments.s665-g84: بزودی is an adverb meaning “quickly” here and modifies the verb. کمر بازکرد is the simple past 3rd‑person singular: بازکرد = “opened / undid,” with کمر (“girdle/belt”) as the object (i.e. “undid his girdle”). از میان is a locative/partitive phrase “from the middle / out of the midst,” specifying how/where he opened it.
segments.s665-g85: بدو is the enclitic/prepositional pronoun “to him / unto him.” داد is the past 3rd‑person singular “gave.” پرمایه زرین together form a compound adjectival description before the noun: پرمایه “substantial/richly endowed” + زرین “golden,” modifying کمر “girdle/belt.” The whole phrase = “(he) gave him a rich golden girdle.”
segments.s665-g86: به هر مهره‌ای is a prepositional phrase “on/at each bead” (به = on/for, هر = each, مهره‌ای = bead/amulett). درنشانده is a past participle meaning “set/inserted” used attributively, and گهر is the head noun “gem/jewel.” The order yields “gems set in every bead.”
segments.s665-g87: خداوند is the nominal subject “the lord/sovereign” (an honorific noun). رز follows as a noun (here lexical; in classical contexts such sequences can be appositive or genitive—context determines precise relation). چون + دید is the common classical temporal construction “when he saw …” (چون introducing the temporal clause) and گفت is the reporting verb “he said,” introducing the quoted or reported content that follows.
segments.s665-g88: که here introduces the content of the saying (equivalent to “that/—”), and کردار بد is the noun phrase “evil deed / bad conduct.” چند in this rhetorical question functions “how (long)/how much” (asking extent or duration). باید is the modal “must/ought,” and نهفت is a verb/participle meaning “be hidden” / “be concealed.” Together they form the question “How long must bad deeds be hidden?”
segments.s665-g33: مر is an archaic benefactive/object pronoun meaning 'to/for me'; او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the object). پدر کرده is a causative/result phrase where کرده (past participle/past form of کردن) means 'made' — literally 'made (him) father (to me)'. پرویز نام places the name before نام (an older/poetic word-order): 'named Parviz' or 'by the name Parviz.'
segments.s665-g34: گهش is an adverb 'occasionally/sometimes'. خواندی is an archaic past form of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call' ('they called him'); it precedes the name phrase in this poetic word order. خسرو شادکام is the name/title given as the object of خواندی ('called Khosrow Shadkam').
segments.s665-g35: نبودی is the negative past of بودن ('was not'); here it governs the following adverbial/phrase. جدا 'apart' and یک زمان 'a single moment' together express 'not apart even for a moment', while از پدر marks the source/other party — 'from his father.' The word order (verb before noun phrase) is poetic but the sense is 'was not separated from his father for a moment.'
segments.s665-g36: پدر نیز is the subject phrase 'the father also'. نشگیفتی is an archaic past form meaning 'was astonished' (derived from شگفتن); از پسر is a prepositional phrase 'at/by the son' giving the cause. The clause means 'the father, too, was amazed at the son.'
segments.s665-g37: چنان بد که is a correlative conjunction 'so... that' introducing the result clause. اسبی 'a horse' is indefinite; ز آخر 'from the rear' is a prepositional phrase ('from the end/behind'); بجست is a past tense verb 'leapt' (ب- prefix in classical forms). The clause reads 'so (bad) that a horse leapt from behind.'
segments.s665-g38: که continues the result clause ('and/which'), with بد used substantively as 'ill/misfortune'. شاه پرویز را is the object ('upon King Parviz', marked by را) and برنشست is the past verb 'alighted/sat upon'. Together: 'which misfortune alighted upon King Parviz.'
segments.s665-g39: سوی is the preposition 'toward'; کشتمند here is a poetic adjective/noun meaning something like 'the slain' or 'of the field of slaughter' (used adjectivally/nominally), so 'toward the slain/field came'. آمد is the simple past 'came'. اسب جوان is the subject phrase 'a young horse'. The line shows flexible poetic order: prepositional phrase + verb + subject.
segments.s665-g40: نگهبان اسب is a genitive compound 'the horse's keeper/guard'. اندر آمد is a compound meaning 'came in/entered', and دوان is a manner adverb 'running/rushed' (literally 'galloping'); together: 'the horse-keeper came in running.'
segments.s665-g193: مگر here functions as an adversative 'rather' introducing an alternative course of action. مردمی جویی is a nominal phrase: مردمی (of the people / popular) modifies جویی (search/pursuit), literally 'the seeking of the people' = 'seeking popular support' or 'seeking the people's good.' و راستی simply coordinates راستی ('uprightness, righteousness') with the preceding nominal phrase; there is no explicit finite verb—the imperative or exhortation is supplied by the surrounding speech.
segments.s665-g194: بدور is an adverbial/compound meaning 'away' / 'apart from' and precedes the verb-like form افگنی. افگنی here is an archaic/poetic verb-form (from افکندن) with an exhortative/optative sense 'cast/throw (away)' (the morphological 2nd‑person form used as a jussive); its object is the coordinated nouns کژی و کاستی. کژی 'crookedness, wrongness' and کاستی 'shortcoming/deficiency' are the objects being cast away.
segments.s665-g195: رهانی is a noun meaning 'release/deliverance' (an action/result). سر کهتران را is a genitive phrase 'the heads (lives) of the lowly' with را marking it as the definite object/topic of the action. ز بد is a prepositional phrase 'from evil' (ز = 'from', بد = 'evil/bad'); the whole segment expresses 'deliverance of the lowly from evil.'
segments.s665-g196: چنان 'so' / 'in such a way' begins a purposive/result clause; کز is the fused form of که + ز 'that/from which' (here introducing a relative/result clause). ره پادشاهان is a possessive/construct 'the way/manner of the kings', and سزد is an impersonal/present jussive 'it befits' or 'is fitting'. Together: 'in such a way that it befits the kings.'
segments.s665-g197: شنیدستی is an older 2nd‑person perfect/present perfect form meaning 'have you heard?'—a rhetorical question here. آن داستان بزرگ is the definite object 'that great tale/story' (آن = 'that', داستان = 'tale/legend', بزرگ = 'great/important'). Word order is typical: verb (question) + object.
segments.s665-g198: که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' linking back to 'داستان'. ارجاسب is a proper name (Arjasp). آن is emphatic/demonstrative 'that' qualifying the name; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/famous'. سترگ is an older/poetic adjective here used as a strong qualifier 'mighty/large' (the given sense list doesn't capture this meaning exactly), so the phrase describes Arjasp as 'that renowned, mighty one.'
segments.s665-g199: بگشتاسب و لهراسب are two proper names joined by و (and). از بهر is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' or 'on account of', followed by دین 'religion'. The whole segment sets the actors and their stated motive: 'Goshtasp and Lohrasp, for the sake of religion.'
segments.s665-g200: چه is an interrogative/exclamatory particle 'what' / 'how' (here 'what ill' or 'what harm'); بد کرد is the simple past compound 'did badly / did harm' (بد modifies کرد). با introduces the partner/target 'with': با آن سواران چین = 'with those horsemen of Chin (China)'. چین is the proper name 'Chin/China' specifying the riders. Word order presents the question 'what harm did they do with those Chinese horsemen?'.
segments.s665-g297: بپرسش گرفت: مرکب فعلی where گرفت functions as a light verb meaning “began/undertook,” and بپرسش is a verbal noun/infinitival form meaning “to ask/about asking.” Together they mean “he began to ask” or simply “he inquired.” اختر دخترش is a genitive phrase (“the star of his daughter/his daughter's star”) placed after the verb phrase in the poetic word order.
segments.s665-g298: که تا چون بود گردش اخترش: که introduces reported content (‘asking/so that’), تا and چون form a poetic question-word cluster meaning roughly “how/what (was)”; بود is the past copula/auxiliary ‘was/would be’; گردش اخترش = “the course/circuit of her star.” The whole segment asks about the nature/course of the daughter's star.
segments.s665-g299: ستاره‌شمر گفت: ستاره‌شمر is the subject (‘the star‑counter/astrologer’). گفت is the simple past of گفتن ‘said’ introducing direct speech. جز نیکویی = “except goodness” (جز used as exception; together with the following negated verb it yields the sense “nothing but goodness”).
segments.s665-g300: نبینی و جز راستی نشنوی: two parallel negative-present/jussive forms. نبینی and نشنوی are negative forms built with نـ + present/optative stem, here functioning as prophetic/predicative negatives: “you will not see… you will not hear…”. With جز preceding the noun phrase they mean “see nothing but …” and “hear nothing but …” so the sense is “you will see only goodness and hear only truth.”
segments.s665-g301: ازین دخت و از شاه ایرانیان: ازین is the older/contractional form of از این ‘from this’; دخت = ‘maiden/daughter’; و از شاه ایرانیان repeats the from‑phrase (‘and from the king of the Iranians’). The two sources combined indicate the parents/lineage of the coming child.
segments.s665-g302: یکی کودک آید چو شیر ژیان: یکی before the noun gives “one/a single” child; کودک آید is the verb phrase ‘a child will come/be born’ (آید = come/appear); چو + simile follows: چو شیر ژیان = “like a lion (in) life/spirit,” i.e. resembling a lion in vigor or temperament. Word order places the simile after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s665-g303: ببالا بلند و ببازو ستبر: the prefix بـ here is instrumental/attributive (“with” or forming an adverbial adjective), so ببالا بلند = “high/tall in height” and ببازو ستبر = “with stout/strong arms.” Adjectives follow the b‑prefix for compressed, poetic description.
segments.s665-g304: به مردی چو شیر و ببخشش چو ابر: به here marks the domain/quality (“in/with regard to”); مردی can mean “man” or “manliness” — here it denotes manhood/valor. چو شیر = “like a lion” (simile for courage). ببخشش is the b‑form of بخشیدن (here nominal/gerundial sense “his granting/generosity”), and چو ابر = “like a cloud” (a conventional image for abundant generosity).
segments.s665-g97: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing this descriptive clause. هم…هم is a correlative pair 'both…and' marking two coordinated predicates: دادده is a substantive/epithet 'giver of justice' (a compound from داد + ده) and بود is the past copula 'was'; و links the two coordinated predicates and the second هم again emphasizes 'also' before دادخواه 'one who seeks justice' used as another predicate noun.
segments.s665-g98: کلاه کیی is a genitival noun phrase 'the Kayanian (کیی) crown/hat' with the modifier after the head. برنهاده is a past participial/compound verb (بر + نهاده, from نهادن) meaning 'has placed / set'. بِماه here is an archaic fused prepositional/simile phrase ('به ماه') meaning 'like the moon' (i.e. set so as to shine like the moon); grammatically it modifies برنهاده.
segments.s665-g99: نکردی is the past negative of کردن ('did not do / did not make') used here with a verb-object nominal (درنگ) to mean 'did not tarry' — classical Persian often places the (negated) verb before its locative adjunct. بَشهر مداین is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + شهر + مداین) meaning 'in/to the city Madā'in' and functions as the locative complement of 'نکردی…درنگ'.
segments.s665-g100: دلاور is a noun/adjective 'valiant, brave' modifying an implied person; سری here functions as a substantive 'head/chief' (an epithet) so 'a valiant chief/leader'. بود is the past copula 'was'. با introduces a circumstantial phrase 'with/possessing' and نام و ننگ is a coordinating pair 'name and (literally) disgrace/shame' — in classical usage this phrase marks attributes carried by the man; the exact evaluative shade of ننگ can be context-dependent in older poetry.
segments.s665-g101: This is a simple enumerative series of temporal nouns joined by repeated و 'and' (بهار, تموز, زمستان, تیر). The final item تیر is a month-name (Tir) here, not 'arrow' — the series gives continuous time periods.
segments.s665-g102: نیاسود is the negative perfect/past form 'did not rest' (ن + آسود). هرمز is the proper name 'Hormoz' and is the logical subject; یل شیرگیر is a titulary noun phrase where یل 'hero' is qualified by شیرگیر 'lion-catcher' (an epithet). Classical Persian allows the verb (نیاسود) to precede its subject for poetic effect.
segments.s665-g103: همی‌گشت is the iterative/imperfective past 'kept going / roamed about'. گرد جهان is a postpositional phrase 'around the world' (گرد = 'around/about' governing جهان). سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'throughout / from end to end' and intensifies the scope of 'roamed'.
segments.s665-g104: همی‌جست is another iterative imperfect 'kept seeking'. در پادشاهی is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the realm/kingdom of' and هنر is the object of seeking here meaning 'art/skill' (i.e. prowess or excellence); together the phrase means he sought skill/merit in matters of kingship.
