segments.s665-g65: بخنجر is an instrumental prepositional phrase ('with a dagger') placed before the verb for emphasis. جدا کرد is the compound past tense verb meaning 'severed / cut apart' (جدا + کرد = 'made apart'); زو is the terse poetic form of از او ('from him') marking source, and گوش و دم lists the two direct parts ('ear and tail') joined by و ('and'). Word order is topical/instrumental → verb → source → objects, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s665-g66: بران here = بر آن (upon that) functioning as a locative adverbial linking the action to a place; کشت‌زاری is the noun 'cultivated field / farmland' modified by the following relative clause. که introduces the relative clause 'which/that', آزرد is the past tense 'hurt / damaged', and سم is the agent/instrument in that clause meaning 'hoof' (the hoof that damaged the field). The whole segment is a locative relative phrase ('upon that field which the hoof had damaged').
segments.s665-g67: همان نیز is a topical modifier meaning 'that same, also' (همان = 'the same'; نیز = 'also'). تاوان is the direct object 'retribution/compensation'. ب + دان here spell out the dative/locative particle (بدان = 'to that'), and دادخواه is the indirect object 'the claimant / avenger' (one who seeks justice). The phrase as a whole means 'that same retribution also to that claimant.'
segments.s665-g68: رسانید is a past tense verb 'he delivered / caused to be sent'. In this poetic line the verb precedes its subject (poetic inversion): خسرو (Khosrow) is the subject performing the action. بفرمان شاه is a postposed agent/instrumental phrase 'by the order of the king' that indicates authority for the action. Together: 'Khosrow, by the king's command, delivered/sent.'
segments.s665-g69: وزان پس is a temporal/discourse link: وزان = 'from that / from then' and پس = 'then/afterwards'. بنخچیر is a predicate noun 'spoils / booty', شد is the past copula 'became', and شهریار (placed after the verb due to poetic word order) is the subject 'the king'. The segment says 'from that time on the king became (one who had) the spoils.'
segments.s665-g70: بیاورد is a present/subjunctive-form verb used generically here ('brings' / 'whoever brings'); in poetry the verb often precedes the subject. هر کس = 'whoever / every person' is the subject, and فراوان شکار is the object phrase 'abundant prey / plentiful game' (فراوان qualifying شکار). The segment expresses a generalization: 'whoever brought abundant game.'
segments.s665-g71: سواری ردی is a noun + postpositive adjective: سواری 'rider/horseman' qualified by ردی 'worthless/lowly' following it (typical Persian adjective placement after the noun in classical poetry). مرد کنداوری is an appositive phrase 'the man Kandauri' (مرد = 'man' followed by the proper name کنداوری). The whole phrase introduces a character: 'a lowly rider, the man Kandauri.'
segments.s665-g72: سپهبدنژادی is a compound epithet meaning 'of sepahbod (military) lineage' and functions as a descriptive/attributive phrase attached to the preceding noun; بلند‌اختری is a poetic epithet literally 'high-starred' (i.e. illustrious or of high fortune). Both are adjectival epithets in apposition to the man just named, giving noble origin despite the earlier 'lowly rider' description.
segments.s665-g289: Word order is subject (من) — object with the Persian definite/object marker (او را) — verbal complex (گزین کردم). گزین is the lexical verb 'to choose' and کرد(م) is the light verb in past tense (I did/I chose), while از دختران is a prepositional phrase 'from among the maidens' that indicates the set from which she was chosen.
segments.s665-g290: نگه داشتم is a past-tense verbal phrase: نگه (a noun 'gaze/keeping') + داشتم (past of داشتن, 'I kept / I had'), so literally 'I kept a gaze.' چشم is in apposition/clarifying the object (my eye / my look). زان (from that) links that gaze to the chosen one, and دیگران ('the others') marks the set contrasted with her — together: 'I kept my eye on that one, apart from the others.'
segments.s665-g291: مرا (here 'to me') appears before گفت (said) — a typical Persian order where the indirect object precedes the verb. خاتون is the subject ('the lady'). که introduces reported speech/command; دیگر گزین is the reported imperative/infinitival instruction 'choose another' (دیگر = 'another/other', گزین = 'choose').
segments.s665-g292: The second که here gives the reason ('for / because'): 'for all five are good.' هر پنج is an idiomatic grouping 'each/all five', خوبند is the present plural copula 'they are good', and و با‌آفرین adds the adjective 'and praiseworthy' (با‌آفرین = deserving praise).
segments.s665-g293: مرا پاسخ این بد is an object‑first construction meaning 'they gave me this bad answer' (مرا marks the experiencer/recipient). که introduces the content of the reply: این بایدم = 'this I must' (بایدم = بایَدَم, 'I must / it is necessary for me'). Note the terse, archaic ordering typical of epic Persian.
segments.s665-g294: چو here functions as 'if/when' (conditional), دیگر 'another', گزینم 'I choose' (first person form of گزین) expresses the hypothetical action, and گزند آیدم = 'harm will come to me' (آیدم literally 'comes to me' — a future/resultative sense). The clause means 'if I choose another, harm will befall me.'
segments.s665-g295: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent'; و connects actions; کنداوران را is the definite/accusative object ('the kandâvarân' — here a group name, glossed in the apparatus as 'investigators/excavators'); بخواند is a past-tense or causative-use verb 'he had them called' / 'he summoned them.' The object marker را attaches to کنداوران.
segments.s665-g296: بر تخت شاهی is a locative prepositional phrase 'upon the royal throne.' به زانو نشاند is a verbal phrase: به زانو ('at/on the knee') + نشاند (archaic past of نشاندن 'to seat, set down'), meaning 'he made (them) sit at his knee.' The omitted object of نشاند is recovered from the previous clause (کنداوران را).
segments.s665-g273: This clause introduces the existence of the girl with the particle 'ورا' (here/there) placed before the noun for poetic emphasis. Word order is inverted compared with prose: normally 'شاه ... دختری ... بود'; 'چون' introduces a simile ('like spring'). The item 'بد' in the line is problematic in the supplied lexicon (it normally means 'bad') and here likely belongs to a compound or name, so its ordinary senses don't apply.
segments.s665-g274: 'سراسر' ('throughout/entire') modifies the following predicate; the fixed combination 'پر از' means 'full of', so 'از' here functions like English 'of'. The coordinating 'و' simply links the nouns 'بوی' (scent), 'رنگ' (color) and 'نگار' (ornament/painting).
segments.s665-g275: Poetic word order places the object phrase first: 'مرا' (me + object marker 'را') followed by location 'در شبستان' and then the verb 'فرستاد' with the agent 'شاه' at the end. 'مرا' is the first‑person object ('me'), realized here orthographically as a prefix/suffix sequence; 'را' is the definite/object marker.
segments.s665-g276: 'برفتم' is the past first‑person verb 'I went' (here written in a poetic form). 'بران' is an adverbial/relational particle meaning 'to/unto that' and links to the following nominal phrase 'نامور پیشگاه' ('renowned presence/court'). The whole clause means the speaker proceeded to that famous court.
segments.s665-g277: The verb 'بیاراستند' is past plural 'they adorned'. 'رخ دختران را' puts the possessed noun phrase before the verb with 'را' marking it as definite/object: 'the faces of the girls' (i.e. they beautified the girls' faces).
segments.s665-g278: 'سر زلف' = 'the head/locks of hair' and the preposition 'بر' ('on/upon') indicates what is placed on the hair. 'بپیراستند' is again past plural 'they adorned/arrayed', so the clause says they put flowers upon the hair/locks.
segments.s665-g279: Here 'مگر' opens a rhetorical/negative question ('surely...?' or 'didn't...?'). 'مادرت' is 'your mother' (مادر + 2nd‑person suffix ت). 'بر سر افسر نداشت' literally 'did not have a helmet on her head' — 'نداشت' is the past form of 'داشت' used negatively ('did not have').
segments.s665-g280: 'همان' ('that/the same') points back to previously imagined finery. The list 'یاره و طوق و گوهر' names ornaments (here 'طوق' and 'گوهر' are 'necklace' and 'jewel'); the final 'نداشت' repeats the negative past 'did not have.' Note that one lexicon sense for 'یاره' in the supplied data does not capture the ornamental meaning in context, so its provided senses don't match exactly.
segments.s665-g345: بگفت is an archaic narrative perfect/past ('he said') used frequently in Shahnameh. این و links what was just said to the following clause (the و here functions as a narrative connector, 'and/then'). جانش ('jan' + enclitic -ش) is the possessed noun 'his life'; برآمد is a pictorial verb 'issued forth/arose' used idiomatically for a soul leaving the body; ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' placed after the verb in poetic order.
segments.s665-g346: This line has inverted, poetic word order: predicative adjectives come before the auxiliary-verb and the subject (انجمن) appears at the end. زار و گریان are predicative adjectives/participial states ('wretched and weeping') and شدند is the past-form of شدن 'became' (they became). انجمن is the subject placed after the verb for metrical effect. The first token بــرو here is an archaic/intensifying element in this phrase (found in classical Persian compounds like بروزار) rather than a literal imperative 'go'; its exact origin is idiomatic/poetic.
segments.s665-g347: شهنشاه is the subject 'the king'. زو is the preposition ز plus the enclitic pronoun او (زو = 'because of him/at him'), an archaic clitic form; در شگفتی is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment', and بماند is the narrative past/preterite sense 'remained' (ب‑ prefix in classical Persian appears in many tenses). Overall: 'The king remained in astonishment because of him.'
segments.s665-g348: به مژگان here means 'by/with (his) eyelashes' (به used instrumentally), and همی is the continuous/iterative particle giving an ongoing sense ('kept on' / 'continually'). خون دل is a poetic compound 'blood of the heart' (i.e. heart's blood / bitter tears), and برفشاند is a pictorial verb 'he scattered/sprinkled' used here in the narrative tense to describe the action. The clause is a vivid, poetic description of his weeping.
segments.s665-g349: به ایرانیان گفت is 'he said/told to the Iranians' (به + indirect object). The sequence مهران ستاد follows: مهران (a proper name) and ستاد (past verb 'he praised') form the reported content or subject of the remark; classical Persian often permits such compact clauses where the syntax of reported speech is economical. Note: گفت here functions as the speech verb and ستاد is another verb reporting the act of praising.
segments.s665-g350: همی‌داشت is a past continuous construction ('kept' / 'was holding'), with همی marking continuity. این راستی‌ها is the direct object 'these truths' (این referring back to what was said), and بیاد is an adverbial/postpositional element meaning 'in memory' or 'for remembrance' (i.e. 'kept in mind / remembered'). The whole clause = 'he kept these truths in memory.'
segments.s665-g351: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. با من here means 'to me' / 'in my presence' (classical usage permits با + person for 'to/with me'), یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' / 'each in turn', and بگفت is the narrative past 'he said/told'. و links the actions, and بمرد is the narrative past 'he died' (classical forms like بمرد are used in narration; the form is archaic compared with modern مرد).
segments.s665-g352: پسندیده here is an epithet meaning 'the beloved/approved one' or 'esteemed' attached to the person; جانش ('his life') is the direct object and به یزدان is the prepositional phrase 'to God'. سپرد is the simple past 'entrusted/handed over'. Together: 'the beloved entrusted his life to God.'
segments.s665-g353: سپاسم is the first-person possessive/verb-like form 'my thanks' used idiomatically as 'I give thanks'. ز (poetic) marks the beneficiary/source 'to/from' so ز یزدان = 'to God' (i.e. 'thanks to God'). کزین = ک+از+این ('from this' / 'that from this'), followed by مرد پیر 'this old man' (مرد = man, پیر = old). The whole phrase strings as 'I give thanks to God that from this old man...'.
segments.s665-g354: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth'. چنین گفتن is a noun-phrase 'such saying/these words' (گفتن here as the verbal noun 'saying'), and ناگزیر modifies it as 'inevitable/compelled'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic meter: literally 'arose such saying inevitable' = 'such words necessarily arose'.
segments.s665-g355: نشان 'a sign/token' is the object. جست is from جستن 'to seek' (here used as the verb stem) and باید is the necessity marker 'must/ought to'; together (inverted for verse) convey 'a sign must be sought'. ز هر مهتری = 'from every مهتری' (هر = every; مهتری = a superior/chief).
segments.s665-g356: اگر ... ار ... here functions as a correlative 'whether ... or ...' (classical usage). اگر مهتری باشد = 'if/whether he be (a) greater/chief' where باشد is the subjunctive/present form 'be'. ار کهتری = 'or (if) lesser'. The clause qualifies from whom the sign should be sought.
segments.s665-g357: بجویید is a plural/imperative or hortative form 'seek/search (you all)'. تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/until'. این بجای آورید is a verbal phrase 'bring this into place' — بجای (in place) + آوردن 'to bring/achieve' — meaning 'accomplish or restore this'. The sequence is a command: 'Seek so that you bring this about.'
segments.s665-g358: همه رنج‌ها را is the definite object 'all the sufferings' (را marks the definite object). به پای آورید is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'bring to the foot/bring to an end' (پای = 'foot/end'); here it commands to put an end to or subdue the hardships. آورید is the plural imperative 'bring'.
segments.s665-g359: یکی = 'a certain' (introducing someone). مهتری here means 'a chief/leader'. نامبردار is an epithet — 'named/renowned' — attached to the person. بود is the past copula 'was'. The segment states existence: 'There was a certain chief, (named) Namabardar/renowned.'
segments.s665-g360: که is the relative pronoun 'who'. بر آخر = 'at/on the rear (lit. upon the end)'; اسب سالار is a compound 'horse-commander' or 'commander of the horses' (سالار = commander). بود is again the past 'was'. The whole relative clause describes his post: 'who was commander at the rear of the horses.'
segments.s665-g305: A string of three adjectives joined by و (and). Word order is attributive: adjectives precede and describe the (implied) person. This is a descriptive nominal phrase standing alone to characterize the subject in the next clause.
segments.s665-g306: Two short clauses: پدر بگذرد uses the verb بگذرد (here narratively 'pass away/die') with پدر as subject; the next clause او بود شهریار is a simple past copular clause 'he was/ became king.' The sequence shows: father died → he (the son) was king. بود is the past copula.
segments.s665-g307: فراوان (adverb/adjective) modifies the verb phrase; ز + noun marks source ('from his father's treasure'). بر‌خورد here is used in the sense 'to obtain/come upon' (poetic usage); word order is Source after فراوان and before the verb, a common Persian order in poetry.
segments.s665-g308: بسی روزگاران is a noun phrase meaning 'many/ample years/times'; ببد is a past copular/auxiliary ('was' / 'became') and نشمرد is the negative-prefixed past ('did not count'). Together they mean he did not reckon or keep account of his many days/years of fortune.
segments.s665-g309: وزان پس is a temporal/consecutive phrase ('and from that / after that'); یکی شاه خیزد is 'a certain king rose' with خیزد (present/subjunctive form used narratively) meaning 'arose/appeared.' The adjective/qualifier سترگ immediately follows to qualify the king (poetic placement).
segments.s665-g310: ز ترکان marks origin ('from the Turks'); the verb (here written بیارد — poetic form of بیاورد) means 'brought/gathered' and takes سپاهی بزرگ as its object ('a great army'). Word order is prepositional phrase → verb → object, common in Persian narrative lines.
segments.s665-g311: بسازد is a narrative/historical past sense of 'to make/arrange/establish' here introducing a content clause with که ('that'); the clause names the targets ایران و شهر یمن as the objects of the resulting action. The conjunction که links the verb of planning to the subordinate clause.
segments.s665-g312: سراسر (entire/throughout) modifies the verb بگیرد (subjunctive/intentional 'to seize/take'); بران 'upon/onto that' points back to the previously mentioned actor or circumstance ('by/on that'), and انجمن is the agent/actor 'assembly/host.' Grammatically this is a subordinate clause expressing the intended result introduced by بسازد که.
segments.s665-g233: "بجز" at the start is the preposition 'except'; it introduces the lone thing left. "کینه ساوه شاهش" is a possessive/genitive sequence 'the grudge/hatred of the king of Saveh' (classical Persian often strings possessor after possessed). "نماند" is the past tense verb 'remained' — here used to say 'there remained nothing except...' so the clause means 'nothing remained except the grudge of the king of Saveh.'
segments.s665-g234: "خرد را" is the direct object with the definite marker را: 'wisdom' as a definite thing being affected. The sequence "به اندیشه اندر" (به + noun + اندر) means 'into/within thought' — an emphatic, somewhat archaic prepositional phrasing. "نشاند" is an archaic, transitive past verb 'set/caused to sit/placed' (literally 'made to sit'); the subject is omitted in classical style and links back to the actor in the context, so the clause reads 'he placed wisdom into thought.'
segments.s665-g235: "یکی" here means 'a certain' (introducing an individual). "بنده بد" is 'a bad servant' (adjective follows the noun). "شاه را" is the definite object marked by را (the king is the object of the forthcoming naming); note classical Persian often places the object before the verb (SOV). "شادکام" begins a series of epithets applied to the king and is syntactically linked to the verb/name that follows in the next segment.
segments.s665-g236: The adjectives "خردمند و بینا و نستوه" are joined by و and list epithets: 'wise, discerning, and distressed.' The final word "نام" (literally 'name') is used elliptically/poetically as the verbal idea 'named/called' — the full action 'he named/called (the king) ...' is expressed by the combination of the preceding clause plus this terminal "نام."
segments.s665-g237: "به شاه جهان" is a prepositional phrase 'to the king of the world' (به + compound title). "گفت" is the simple past 'said' introducing direct speech. "انوشه" appears as the vocative/name being addressed. "بدی" is the noun 'evil/harm' and participates in the following optative wish; here it is introduced with the speech verb and continues into the next clause.
segments.s665-g238: "ز تو" (poetic 'from you') + "دور" ('far') together form 'far from you.' "بادا" is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' (a wish). "همیشه" is the adverb 'always.' The noun "بدی" at the end is the subject of the wish ('evil/harm'); the arrangement yields the formulaic blessing/wish 'May evil be far from you always.'
segments.s665-g239: بپرسید is an exhortative/imperative form ('ask! / one should ask') placed before the modal باید by poetic inversion; together بپرسید باید = 'one must ask' or 'you must ask'. ز marks the target/source of the asking ('of/from'). مهران is the personal name; ستاد here functions as an epithet or adjective derived from a past-tense form (literally 'praised' or 'established') attached to مهران, so ز مهران ستاد = 'of Mehrān Setād' (i.e. Mehrān with that epithet).
segments.s665-g240: که introduces the subordinate (indirect) question 'what ...'; از روزگاران = 'of/from the ages' (روزگاران = 'ages, former times'). چه is the interrogative 'what'; دارد is the present-tense verb 'he has' (possesses); بیاد is a frozen classical phrase meaning 'in memory' or 'as a recollection' and here completes the idea 'what he has in memory' = 'what he remembers'. Word order چه دارد بیاد = 'what he has (in) memory'.
segments.s665-g329: Word order is inverted: the prepositional phrase of recipient 'به نوشین‌روان' ('to Nushin‑ravan') comes first, followed by the verb 'داد' ('gave'), then the temporal/connecting particle 'پس' ('then'), and finally the object 'دخترش' ('his daughter') marked by possession. In Classical Persian narrative the verb often appears before the subject, and here the recipient is fronted for emphasis.
segments.s665-g330: 'که' introduces a relative clause qualifying 'دخترش' (’his daughter’). The clause literally reads 'that from his daughters bad Afser' — an appositive/identifying structure: 'افسرش' is a proper name (with possessive ’-ش’ = 'his Afser') and 'بدی' is predicative ('bad/worst'). The order places predicate before the name for poetic effect; the clause means 'who, of his daughters, Afser was the worst.'
segments.s665-g331: The verb 'پذیرفتم' ('I accepted/took') starts the clause; the object 'او را' ('her/him' with the object marker را) follows, and the subject 'من' ('I') is stated explicitly for emphasis. The prepositional phrase 'از بهر شاه' is an archaic/formal expression meaning 'for the sake of the king' ('از' + 'بهر' = 'for').
segments.s665-g332: 'چو' here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; 'آن کرده بد' is a verbless or participial clause meaning 'that bad deed having been done' (آن = that; کرده = done; بد = bad/ill). The main verb 'بازگشتم' ('I returned/turned back') follows, and 'به راه' ('on the road' or 'on my way') is a locative adjunct. The clause thus reads 'when that bad thing was done, I returned on my way.'
segments.s665-g333: The verb 'بیاورد' (narrative past 'he brought') governs the noun phrase 'چندی گهرها' ('several gems' — quantity word before the plural noun) and the source marked by the poetic preposition 'ز' ('from') plus 'گنج' ('treasure'). Classical Persian often uses 'ز' instead of modern 'از' in poetry.
segments.s665-g334: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying 'گهرها' ('gems'). 'ما یافتیم' = 'we found/obtained', and the gerundial construction 'از کشیدنش رنج' literally 'suffering from its being pulled' uses the verb stem + possessive suffix '-ش' to mean 'their extraction' (کشیدن = to pull/extract) followed by 'رنج' ('hardship/suffering'). Together: 'which we obtained after suffering in pulling/extracting them.'
segments.s665-g335: 'همان' ('the same' / 'he') functions as the subject; 'تا' here means 'as far as' and combines with 'لب رود جیحون' ('the brink/edge of the Jeyhun river') to form a goal/directional phrase. The verb 'براند' (3rd‑person singular of راندن) means 'drove' or 'drove along/away' — so the clause says he drove (something/someone) up to the river's edge.
segments.s665-g336: This clause contains 'جهان‌بین خود را' as the object: 'جهان‌بین' is an epithet ('world‑seer' / 'world‑wise'), and 'خود را' can be reflexive ('himself') or possessive/emphatic ('his ...'). The particle 'بکشتی' here means 'onto/by a ship' (classical prepositional phrase 'به کشتی' written together), and the verb 'نشاند' is the causative/past 'set/placed/caused to sit' (archaic past form). So the sense is 'he placed his/world‑wise one on the ship' — the construction allows an ambiguity whether he seated himself or set his attendant/title on board.
segments.s665-g377: جهاندیده modifies روی and is an adjective compound meaning 'worldly-wise' or 'having seen the world'; it precedes the noun it qualifies (typical in poetry). روی here means 'face' (the direct object). شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor/sovereign' and دید is the past-tense verb 'saw/ beheld' (3rd singular). The object phrase is fronted (OSV order), so literal order is 'world-wise face the emperor saw.'
segments.s665-g378: بران is a prepositional/pronominal particle here meaning 'upon that' or 'thereon'; نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying the implied person; آفرین functions as a noun/exclamation 'acclamation, praise' rather than the verb 'to create'; گسترید is the past of گستراندن 'to spread/extend' and here means 'poured out' or 'bestowed' (3rd singular). Together they form 'he poured out acclamations upon that renowned one.'
segments.s665-g379: نگه + کرد forms a compound verb 'looked' (نگه 'gaze' + کرد auxiliary). شاه is the grammatical subject 'the king'. اندرو یک زمان is an adverbial phrase: اندروی (here 'at him / toward him') plus یک زمان 'for a moment' (زمان = moment), so 'looked at him for a moment.' Word order places the verb before the subject (poetic flexibility).
segments.s665-g380: نبودش is negative past 'there was not / he did not have it' with the enclitic -ش referring back to 'thought' or 'suspicion'; بدو 'toward him / of him' pinpoints the object of the feeling. جز = 'except'; به نیکی گمان is a frozen phrase 'a good opinion / favorable suspicion' (به + noun = 'in a ... way'). So the clause means 'he had no thought of him except a good one' i.e. he thought only well of him.
segments.s665-g381: نشان‌های مهران is a genitive construction 'Mehran's signs/insignia' (نشان‌های = plural 'signs'). ستاد is a past-tense verb meaning 'he praised' (here 'praised' or 'commended'). اندروی is an adverb meaning 'inwardly / to himself' (i.e. privately or in his heart). The clause reads 'he praised Mehran's insignia inwardly.'
segments.s665-g382: This is a sequence of simple past verbs joined by و: بدید 'he saw' (or 'upon seeing'), و بخندید 'and he laughed', و شد 'and he became'. تازه‌روی is a descriptive predicate meaning 'fresh-faced / renewed in countenance' (an adjectival/nominal form). The clause therefore narrates: 'he saw, laughed, and became fresh-faced.'
segments.s665-g383: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that / thereafter'. بپرسید is the past-tense verb 'he asked/questioned'. و is the conjunction 'and'. بنواختش is the past of بناواختن meaning 'he struck (a string) / played' with the enclitic -ش 'him/it' (here 'played for him' or 'struck up a tune for him'). So: 'After that he questioned him and played (for) him.'
segments.s665-g384: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one'; نامور is the adjective 'renowned'; جایگه (جا + گاه) means 'place/position' or 'rank'; ساختش is the past of ساختن 'made/established' with the enclitic -ش 'him' (i.e. 'made him [into]'). Together: 'he made him a renowned place/position' — idiomatically 'he appointed him to a famous post.'
segments.s665-g281: 'از ایشان' is a prepositional phrase placed frontally meaning 'of/from them'; 'جز' ('except') introduces the exception; the sequence 'او دخت خاتون' is a pronominal reference ('she') followed by the appositive noun phrase 'دختِ خاتون' ('maiden/ lady'). The negative past verb 'نبود' ('was not') scopes over the whole clause: 'There was not (among them) anyone except her.'
segments.s665-g282: The preposition 'به' is used adverbially here to mark accompaniment/quality ('with' / 'in'); three coordinated nouns 'پیرایه و رنگ و افسون' ('ornament, color/makeup, and charm') form the list of qualities, and the clause ends with the negative past 'نبود' ('was not/had not'). Together: 'she had no ornament, color, or enchantment.'
segments.s665-g283: The clause begins with the relative/conjunction 'که' linking to the previous idea ('for/that'); 'خاتون' is the subject and 'چینی' is a postpositive descriptor ('a Chinese lady'). The poem uses the poetic preposition 'ز' ('from') with the title 'فغفور' (the Byzantine emperor) and finishes with the past copula 'بود' ('was'): 'the lady was Chinese and came from the emperor.'
segments.s665-g284: 'به گوهر' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in essence' (گوهر here = 'essence'); the construction 'ز کردار بد دور بود' literally 'was distant from bad conduct' uses 'ز' = 'from' + noun phrase 'کردار بد' ('bad conduct') and the past 'بود' to say she was far removed from ill behavior. The clause therefore qualifies her character.
segments.s665-g285: The particle 'همی' is a classical intensifier/continuative placed before the verb; 'مادرش را' ('his/her mother' with object marker 'را') is the affected person, and 'جگر' is used figuratively as 'heart' (seat of feeling). 'زان' = 'from/for that reason' links to the preceding fact, and the past verb 'بخست' expresses the figurative action ('her heart was broken' / 'she was heart-stricken').
segments.s665-g286: The conjunction 'که' introduces the content/reason clause: 'that the child ...'; 'فرزند' is the theme, 'جایی' ('in a place' / 'somewhere') is adverbial, and 'شود' is the classical third-person (subjunctive/optative) form of 'شد' meaning 'become' or 'might be.' 'دوردست' is the predicate adjective 'far-off.' The sense is purpose/result: 'that the child would be placed far away.'
segments.s665-g287: The copular predicate 'دژم بود' ('was sullen/grim') appears first for emphasis, followed by 'زان' ('because of that' / 'therefore') and the noun phrase 'دختر پارسا' ('the pious daughter') which is the one affected. Word order (predicate before noun) is a common poetic inversion: 'therefore the pious daughter was sullen.'
segments.s665-g288: The nominal/verb phrase 'گسی کردن' uses the indefinite 'گسی' ('some/one') plus the light verb 'کردن' to indicate that someone performed the action; 'از خانه پادشا' ('from the king's house') is a prepositional phrase giving the source/location. In classical narrative this compact form describes that someone took/removed her from the king's household—the full finite verb/subject is left implicit.
segments.s665-g361: This is an exclamatory nominal phrase in archaic/poetic order. کجا here functions like English “what a / how” (exclamatory), راد‌فرخ is a proper name in apposition, and بدی نام اوی literally = “Badi, his name” where نام is the noun “name” and اوی is the old enclitic possessive “his”; the copula (was) is omitted as is common in Persian.
segments.s665-g362: همه شادی شاه is a genitive phrase “all / the whole joy of the king.” بد کام اوی is the predicate: here functioning as a compound idea of being (to) the king’s satisfaction — کام = “satisfaction/desire,” and اوی = “his” (referring back to the person named). The small copula linking subject and predicate is omitted (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s665-g363: بیامد is a past-tense verb “he came,” بر شاه is the prepositional phrase “to the king” (بر = toward/to), and گفت introduces reported speech “(he) said.” این نشان is the demonstrative + noun “this token/mark” (object or topic of what he said). Word order is S–Adv–V typical of the epic register.
segments.s665-g364: که is the relative pronoun “which/that” linking back to نشان. داد is the past verb “gave.” این ستوده is a noun phrase where ستوده is a past-participial epithet “praised” modifying این (this praised one). به introduces the indirect object (“to/for”) and گردن‌کشان is the object “neck-cutters / rebels” (an epithet for insurgents); the whole clause = “that this praised one gave to the rebels.”
segments.s665-g365: ز is the poetic preposition “from.” The names that follow are given in apposition/lineage: the repeated بهرام and the compound پورگشسب (here functioning as a patronymic/epithet). In epic style names and patronymics are strung together without extra linking words.
segments.s665-g366: This is a nominal phrase describing the man: سواری is the head noun “rider/horseman,” سرافراز is a postpositive adjective “proud/triumphant,” و joins the coordinated modifier پیچنده which is a present-participial adjective “twisting/turning” that takes اسب (“horse”) as its object — literal: “a rider, proud and twisting (his) horse.”
segments.s665-g367: ز اندیشه من is a prepositional phrase “from my thought” (ز = from, اندیشه من = my thought). بخواهد گذشت is a verb cluster: بخواهد is an auxiliary/modal meaning “will / is about to / may” and گذشت (past-form) here yields a future/impersonal sense “will pass / be beyond.” Together they express an action/judgement moving beyond the speaker’s thought (poetic, somewhat idiomatic).
segments.s665-g368: ندیدم is the past negative “I did not see.” چنو (= چنان) is the demonstrative/adverb “such / like so,” marking degree. مرزبانی is the noun “border-guarding / border watch” and به دشت is the locative prepositional phrase “on/in the plain” (به + دشت). The clause therefore: “I have not seen such border-guarding in the plain.”
segments.s665-g313: ازو is a fronted prepositional/causal element ('because of him'/'from him') introducing the reason; شاه ایران is the subject noun phrase ('the King of Iran'); شود is a 3rd‑person singular present/classical verbal form 'becomes' (شد + -ه form) and links to the predicate adjective دردمند, which describes the subject. The enclitic/wording is typical of classical/poetic Persian where the causal particle precedes the clause.
segments.s665-g314: بترسد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive form of ترسیدن with the prosthetic بـ common in classical Persian, meaning 'he fears'; ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / from' used here as 'because of'; پیروز بخت بلند is a nominal phrase where پیروز ('victorious') qualifies بخت ('fortune/fate') and بلند ('high/lofty') further qualifies the noun, so the whole phrase means 'the high/victorious fortune' which is the object/reason of the fearing verb. The subject (he) is carried over from the previous clause (the Shah).
segments.s665-g315: یکی is an indefinite introducer ('a certain one'/'someone'); کهتری is an adjectival noun meaning 'lesser' or 'of lower rank'; باشدش is the verb باشد + enclitic -ش ('it/he is to him' / 'he has/there is to him') where -ش refers back to a previously understood person/context; دوردست is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'far‑off' describing location or remoteness. The clause structure is typical of epic diction where predicates and possessive clitics condense information.
segments.s665-g316: سواری is the head noun 'a rider, horseman'; the following adjectives سرافراز ('proud/honored') and the compound مهترپرست ('one who flatters/worships the mahter/chief' = 'sycophant, obsequious to his chief') qualify that rider. Adjectives follow the noun here, a common poetic word order, and مهترپرست is a compound agentive describing the rider's attitude toward his lord.
segments.s665-g317: ببالا is an adverbial/locative adverb 'upward/on high' setting posture; دراز is an adjective 'long/tall' coordinated by و 'and' to the next phrase; به اندام خشک uses the preposition به ('with/of') plus اندام ('figure/frame') and خشک ('dry/lean') to mean 'with a thin/gaunt build.' The adjective خشک here is poetic for 'lean/gaunt' rather than strictly 'dry.'
segments.s665-g318: به گرد is a prepositional phrase 'around/about'; سرش is 'his head' (سر + possessive clitic ش); جعد مویی is a noun phrase 'a curl/lock of hair' where جعد ('curl') qualifies مویی ('a strand/hair'), and چو مشک is a simile 'like musk' modifying that curl. Word order places the possessor after the head (سرش) and ends with the simile, a typical poetic sequence.
segments.s665-g319: سخن‌آوری is a noun 'eloquence/oratory' used here as an appositive trait; جلد is used as 'skin/complexion' (جلد, skin/hide) and is coordinated with بینی بزرگ ('a large nose') by و. The phrase lists physical and verbal attributes in apposition to the person described.
segments.s665-g320: This is a series of adjectival descriptors joined by و: سیه‌چرده ('dark‑complexioned'), تندگوی ('sharp‑tongued' or quick of speech), and سترگ (literally 'eye' used adjectivally here as 'large‑eyed' or 'with a commanding gaze'). In poetic Persian such nouns can function adjectivally to paint features succinctly.
segments.s665-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is simple past 3rd‑person 'he heard' and takes پرویز as its logical subject here (word order is verb before subject, common in epic/Ferdowsi). پوزش‑کنان is an adverbial participle 'apologizing / making excuse' that modifies the subject's manner of hearing or acting.
segments.s665-g58: برانگیخت is past (3rd‑person) used transitively/intransitively as 'roused/summoned/caused to rise' with an implied subject (here Parviz). از هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' (از = 'from', هر = 'every', سویی = 'side/direction'). مهتران is the plural 'the chiefs/elders' (poetic/archaic formation meaning 'great ones').
segments.s665-g59: به نزد پدر is a motion/dative phrase 'to the presence of his father' (به = 'to', نزد = 'in the presence of', پدر = 'father'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that' and is followed by the subjunctive ببخشد 'he may forgive' (ببخشد is the present/subjunctive form used for purpose). گناه is the direct object 'sin/fault' of ببخشد.
segments.s665-g60: نبرد here is a noun ('fight/struggle' / verbal noun) meaning 'battle' or 'struggle'. دم و گوش form a coordinated noun phrase 'tail (lit. “دم”) and ear' specifying what the fight concerns, with اسب سیاه as the restricting noun phrase 'of the black horse'. Grammatically the whole phrase names the contested matter or scene: 'the fight over the tail and ear of the black horse.'
segments.s665-g61: برآشفت is past 3rd‑person 'became angry / was agitated'. ازان پس is a temporal connective 'thereupon / after that' (ازان = 'from that'; پس = 'then/after'). برو here functions as a narrative verb 'went'/'moved on' (classical epic usage) or as part of the clause introducing the king's action; شهریار is the subject 'the king'.
segments.s665-g62: بتندی is an adverbial/verb form meaning 'quickly / hastily' or 'with vehemence' (tendency/force). بزد is past 'he struck / gave' and with بانگ (a concrete noun 'cry, shout') forms the idiom 'بزد بانگ' = 'he uttered a cry' (i.e. made a loud outcry). بر marks the target of the cry ('toward/at') and پیشکار is the target noun 'the attendant/servant'.
segments.s665-g63: موکل شد is a passive/perfective construction 'was entrusted / was made responsible'. از بیم is a prepositional phrase 'out of fear of / because of fear of' (از = 'out of', بیم = 'fear'). هرمز is a proper name (here the deity or person Hormoz), and دوان is a participial/epithetical form meaning 'running / galloping' (it can function as a verbal adjective describing swift action or the one who runs).
segments.s665-g64: بدان is an older/poetic compound functioning as 'to that / toward that' (ب = 'to/into' plus a demonstrative element). کشت here is the noun 'cultivation/plot/field' (in epic diction کشت can denote a patch of planted ground). نزدیک is the preposition/adverb 'near' qualifying the location, and اسب جوان is the noun phrase 'the young horse' that marks the reference point for the location.
segments.s665-g265: بخواهندگی من is a prepositional/agentive phrase meaning “by/at my desire/bidding” (بخواهندگی = ‘by the will/request’ + من = ‘my/I’). بدم here functions as an archaic first‑person verb form “I sent/placed” (classical contraction of بدهم/دادم) and پیشرو is a noun complement meaning “vanguard/forerunner” — together: “At my bidding I put (him/them) forward as vanguard.”
segments.s665-g266: This chunk gives the number and source of the men. صد و شست is a compound numeral (“one hundred and sixty” in older Persian usage). مرد is the noun “men/warriors” and از دلیران locates them ‘from among the brave’; گو at the end is a particle/verb meaning “say/call” or functions reportively — here it finalizes the statement (literally ‘so say’).
segments.s665-g267: پدرت is the noun پدر ‘father’ with the 2nd‑person suffix -ت = ‘your father’. آن جهاندار دانا و راست is a descriptive noun phrase: آن = ‘that/the’, جهاندار = ‘sovereign/ruler’, دانا = ‘wise’, و راست = ‘and upright/just’. The whole phrase modifies ‘your father’ (“your father, that wise and upright sovereign”).
segments.s665-g268: ز خاقان پرستارزاده نخواست is the predicate: ز = poetic ‘from/of’, خاقان = ‘the khagan’, پرستارزاده = ‘a child born of a nurse/wet‑nurse’ (an expression for low or nurse‑born lineage), and نخواست is past negative of خواستن ‘(he) did not want/accept’. Together: ‘(he) did not desire/accept from the khagan a nurse‑born (person).’
segments.s665-g269: مرا گفت is the reporting phrase ‘he said to me’ (مرا = ‘to me’ as object, گفت = ‘said/told’). جز دخت خاتون مخواه is direct reported speech: جز = ‘except’, دخت خاتون = ‘the daughter of the khatun’ (دخت = daughter/maiden + خاتون = lady/consort), and مخواه is the negative imperative ‘do not seek/ask for’. So: “He told me: seek none but the khatun’s daughter.”
segments.s665-g270: نزیبد پرستار در پیشگاه is a short clause giving the reason: نزیبد (from زیبیدن) = ‘it is not fitting / does not befit’, پرستار = ‘nurse/wet‑nurse (or nurse‑born person)’, در پیشگاه = ‘in the presence of / before the court’. Together: ‘a nurse‑born woman is not fitting before the court.’
segments.s665-g271: برفتم is an archaic past first‑person verb ‘I went / I proceeded’ (classical compound of رفتن). به نزدیک خاقان چین = ‘to/near the khagan of China’ (به = ‘to/toward’, نزدیک = ‘near/close to’, خاقان چین = ‘the khagan of China’). So: ‘I went up near the Chinese khagan.’
segments.s665-g272: به شاهی برو خواندم آفرین strings together motion and action: به شاهی (literally ‘to/at the kingship/throne’), برو is an older/imperative or narrative motion ‘I went up/approached’, خواندم = ‘I read/recited/sang’, and آفرین = the exclamation ‘Afarin’ (praise). The sequence means ‘I went up to the throne and recited praise (“Afarin”).’
segments.s665-g209: همان شهرها را که بگرفت شاه is an object phrase plus a relative clause. همان (demonstrative) precedes شهرها (plural 'cities') to mean 'those same cities'. را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to the object. که introduces the relative clause 'که بگرفت شاه' (= 'which the king captured'), where بگرفت is a past tense verb 'took/seized' and شاه, the subject, follows the verb — a common word-order in classical Persian.
segments.s665-g210: سپارم بدو بازگردد ز راه contains two linked clauses. سپارم is first‑person (literary) meaning 'I will entrust / I hand over' and is followed by the indirect object بدو ('to him'). بازگردد is a subjunctive/optative used like an instruction ('let him/the messenger return' or simply 'return'), and ز راه is the poetic prepositional phrase 'by the road' (ز = از). The sequence expresses: I will hand them to him; (you) return by the road.
segments.s665-g211: فرستاده‌ای جست گرد و دبیر is a compact clause: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a messenger' (formed from the participle), جست + گرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'searched about / sought out' (جست here = 'seek', گرد = 'about/around'), and و دبیر connects the following noun 'and a secretary/scribe.' The verb جست governs the action of seeking; word order is noun + verb phrase + conjunction + noun.
segments.s665-g212: خردمند و گویا و دانش‌پذیر are three coordinated adjectives modifying the preceding دبیر. The adjectives follow the noun and are joined by و ('and'): خردمند = 'wise', گویا here = 'eloquent' (not 'apparent'), and دانش‌پذیر = 'teachable / receptive to learning'.
segments.s665-g213: به قیصر چنین گوی is an imperative clause 'Say this to Caesar.' به قیصر = 'to Caesar' (prepositional phrase), چنین = 'thus / in this way', and گوی is the verb 'say' (archaic/poetic imperative form). کز introduces the content clause 'that ...' and is followed by the phrase شهر روم 'the city of Rome' which begins the reported content.
segments.s665-g214: نخواهم دگر باژ آن مرز و بوم is the content clause 'I will not any more [make] a cry against that border and land.' نخواهم is the negated first‑person form meaning 'I will not / I do not want to', with دگر 'anymore/again' as an adverb. باژ is a noun 'cry/noise' (here: a hostile shout/commotion), and آن مرز و بوم = 'that frontier and homeland.' The word order places the negated verb first, followed by adverb and object phrase.
segments.s665-g215: تو هم پای در مرز ایران منه is an admonition. تو = 'you', هم = 'also/too', پای در مرز ایران is a locative phrase 'your foot in the border of Iran' (an idiom meaning to remain at the frontier), and منه here is an archaic prohibitive 'do not' (a negative imperative). The clause thus tells the addressee not to leave or remove his footing from Iran's border.
segments.s665-g216: چو خواهی که مه باشی و روزبه is a conditional clause: چو = 'if/when', خواهی = 'you wish', که introduces the subordinate clause, باشی is the subjunctive/potential 'you be' (you become), and روزبه is a proper name. مه here appears to be a personal/name reference (or a poetic epithet) paired with روزبه; the whole means 'if you wish to be Mah and Ruzbeh' — i.e. to join or be like those persons.
segments.s665-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause; ده سال شد is a verblike phrase meaning 'became ten years' (i.e. 'reached ten years') where شد is the past/become verb; پادشاهیش is the possessive/subject 'his kingship/reign' (the -ش = his) and راست is an adjectival/poetic intensifier meaning something like 'complete/true' attached to 'پادشاهی' (poetic word order places the time clause before the noun).
segments.s665-g106: ز هر کشور introduces source/extent 'from every country/realm'; آواز here means a raised sound/cry or 'call' (a public voice/rumor) and بدخواه is an attributive noun 'ill-wisher / enemy'; خاست is the past/preterite 'arose' (classical literary past). The phrase lists the origin and nature of the outcry.
segments.s665-g107: بیامد is the simple past 'came'; ز راه هری ساوه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/route of the Hari to Saveh' (poetic omission of a connective between 'هری' and 'ساوه' — these are place-names in sequence); شاه is the subject placed after the verb by poetic inversion (verb‑subject order is common in this style).
segments.s665-g108: This is a coordination of accompaniments introduced by repeated با 'with' (با کوس و گنج و سپاه). ابا at the line start is an archaic/poetic particle here functioning like an emphatic or variant of با meaning 'with' (poetic duplication is not uncommon); the list names companions/resources: پیل 'elephants', کوس 'war-drum(s)', گنج 'treasure', سپاه 'army'.
segments.s665-g109: گر is the conditional 'if'; از لشکر ساوه is a genitive/source phrase 'of/from the army of Saveh'; گیری is the present/subjunctive/imperative-form of گرفتن used after گر (classical Persian often omits the prefix ب-), here 'you take' or 'you reckon/take (the number)'; شمار is the object 'the number/count'.
segments.s665-g110: برو is an imperative or hortative 'go (and ...)' introducing the action to perform; چارصد بار literally 'four-hundred times' (بار = 'times/occasions' here); بشمر is the present/imperative of شمردن 'count' (classical form without an initial ب-): together they give 'go and count four hundred times a thousand' — i.e. 400×1000 = 400,000 (the following context supplies the multiplier 'هزار').
segments.s665-g111: ز marks origin/amount 'of/from'; پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephants' and جنگی here is an adjective 'war-' or 'military', so پیلان جنگی = 'war‑elephants'; the written numbers 'هزار و دویست' immediately following mean 'one thousand and two hundred' (1,200) — this is a numeral phrase attached to the elephants.
segments.s665-g112: تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you said' (گفتی = you said); مگر here is the rhetorical interrogative 'isn't it?/surely not?' introducing the quotation; بر زمین راه نیست is a literal negated locative clause 'there is no way/path on the ground' (بر = on/upon, زمین = ground, راه = road/path, نیست = does not exist). The whole segment records what the addressee said as a rhetorical question.
segments.s665-g337: This is a compact past-tense clause in poetic order: a prepositional phrase 'ز جیحون' ('from Jihun/Amu Darya') opens, followed by the noun phrase 'دلی پر ز غم' ('a heart full of grief'), and the past verb 'بازگشت' ('he returned') is placed at the end as is normal in Persian. 'پر ز غم' is the fixed poetic expression 'full of grief'. The subject is omitted (third person).
segments.s665-g338: Another short clause continuing the idea: 'ز فرزند' ('from his/that child') is the prepositional phrase, 'با درد' ('with pain') is an adverbial phrase of manner, 'انباز' is an archaic/uncertain modifier describing his condition (see note), and 'گشت' is a past verb placed at the end meaning 'he went/turned/returned'. The line uses two near-synonymous verbs ('بازگشت' and 'گشت') for poetic emphasis.
segments.s665-g339: 'کنون' ('now') opens the line; 'آنچ دیدم' is a relative construction meaning 'that which I saw', with the past verb 'دیدم'. 'بگفتم' ('I said/I have told') follows and 'همه' ('all/everything') is the object—so the literal order is 'Now that-which I saw I said all', i.e. 'Now I have told everything I saw.' Both verbs are past and the speaker describes a completed action.
segments.s665-g340: This is a destination/beneficiary phrase: 'به پیش' = 'to/in the presence of', followed by an honorific compound 'جهاندار شاه' ('sovereign king') and 'رمه' ('the throng/host') in apposition. The whole phrase means 'before the sovereign king and his host' (i.e. addressed to the king and the assembled people).
segments.s665-g341: 'ازین کشور' ('from this country/land') is a prepositional phrase; note the orthographic split 'از'+'ین' (here treated as the variant 'این'). 'این مرد را' ('this man' + object marker 'را') marks the person to be searched for, and 'باز‌جوی' is a noun/agent meaning 'inquirer' or a verb-form used elliptically as an instruction 'search for' (in poetry nouns and imperatives can be compacted). The clause is therefore an instruction or request to seek this man.
segments.s665-g342: 'بپوینده' is a present-participial/adjectival form 'seeking' (describing the man or his state). 'شاید' ('perhaps') hedges the statement and 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'گویی بپوی' — here an idiomatic/peremptory formula: 'گویی' is used in a performative sense ('so that one might say' / 'say'), and 'بپوی' is an imperative 'seek! / go in search of!' Thus the line offers a conjecture and issues the command 'seek him.' Note: 'گویی' is somewhat idiomatic/archaic in this context.
segments.s665-g343: A causal/explanatory clause introduced by 'که' ('for/that'): 'پیروزی شاه' ('the king's victory') is the subject, 'بر دست اوست' ('is upon/in his hand') is a possessive idiom meaning 'is under his control' or 'depends on him.' Word order follows Persian norm with the copula 'ست' at the end.
segments.s665-g344: This clause is elliptical and somewhat archaic: 'بدشمن ممان' can be read as an admonition 'do not make (him) an enemy' or 'do not oppose' (with 'ممان' functioning as a negative/imperative particle + verb-root meaning 'oppose/refuse'); 'این سخن‌گر' means 'this speaker/orator' and 'بدوست' appears here as a proper name or uncertain epithet. The precise syntax is compacted for poetic effect, so the sense is roughly 'do not make an enemy of this speaker, Bedoost.'
segments.s665-g249: بپرسیدم is a 1st-person past tense verb meaning 'I asked' and heads the clause. از introduces the person being asked ('from / of'); پیر مهران is a noun phrase in which پیر (an epithet 'elder/old') precedes the name مهران. ستاد here appears as an additional word after the name (likely an appositive or an element of the name/description) but does not fit the supplied verbal senses as used as a proper name or epithet in this line.
segments.s665-g250: که introduces an indirect interrogative clause ('that / … where/what…') and governs the following clause. از روزگاران is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the ages'; چه is the interrogative 'what'; داری is the 2nd/3rd‑person-present form 'you/he have(s)' used here as the main predicate of the indirect question; بیاد is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'in memory' so the literal order is 'from the ages what do you have in-memory' → 'what do you remember of the ages.'
segments.s665-g251: چنین داد پاسخ is inverted, idiomatic word order: چنین 'thus' + داد (here the verb 'gave') + پاسخ 'answer', i.e. 'thus he gave answer'. که following it begins the quoted/reported speech; شاه جهان is a vocative/title combination 'king of the world' used to address the ruler.
segments.s665-g252: اگر is the conditional 'if'. پرسدم appears here as an archaic/poetic first‑person form in the protasis functioning as 'if you ask / if I am asked' in context (classical usage allows such flexibilities); بازگویم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive/future‑oriented 'I will recount / I shall tell'; نهان (placed after the verb) functions adjectivally/adverbially 'the hidden' or 'secret things' — so the clause means 'if [you] ask, I will recount the hidden.'
segments.s665-g253: شهنشاه is a grand title 'king of kings / emperor' and is the subject. فرمود here takes the nuance 'commanded/ordered' (فرمود + تا introduces a purpose/result clause). تا is a clause‑linker meaning 'so that / in order that'; در زمان is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the time' specifying when the commanded event should occur.
segments.s665-g254: بشد is an intransitive past/optative form 'it became / there came to be' (here used impersonally to introduce the happening). نزد او is a locative/presence phrase 'to/near him'; نامداری is a substantive/adjectival 'the famed / renown' used as an epithet; دمان in this context functions as a proper name (a person, 'Doman') rather than the literal senses listed, so the supplied lexical senses do not match the usage.
segments.s665-g255: تن پیر is a noun phrase where تن (literally 'body') is used to mean 'the person' and پیر qualifies it as 'the old man'; ازان کاخ is a prepositional phrase 'from that palace' (ازان = 'from that'); برداشتند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they took / removed', with the object (the old man) preceding the prepositional phrase.
segments.s665-g256: به مهد اندرون is a destination phrase 'into/ to the inner cradle/inner chamber' with به marking direction and اندرون qualifying location; تیز is an adverb here 'quickly / swiftly' modifying the verb; بگذاشتند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they placed' (implicit object 'him').
segments.s665-g257: چو آمد = 'when came' (چو = 'when' used as a subordinating particle, آمد past tense 'came'). بر شاه = 'before/to the king' (بر = toward/to, poetic preposition). مرد کهن = 'an old man' (مرد = man, کهن = old/ancient). The verb appears before subject and object by poetic inversion: 'when came to the king an old man.'
segments.s665-g258: دلی پر ز دانش = 'a heart full of knowledge' (دلی here functioning as 'heart/heartful', پر = 'full (of)', ز = poetic genitive 'of/from', دانش = 'knowledge'). سری پرسخن = 'a head full of speech' or 'one given to speech' (سری = 'head', پرسخن = adjectival/nominal compound 'much-speaking, talkative'). Both phrases are adjectival appositions describing the مرد کهن.
segments.s665-g259: بپرسید = 'asked' (past tense, 3rd sing.). هرمز is the subject ('Hormoz'); ز مهران ستاد = 'having praised Mehran' (ز = 'of/from/about' marking the object/person praised; ستاد = 'he praised'). Poetic word order places the verbs and modifiers around the name: Hormoz both praised Mehran and then asked.
segments.s665-g260: کزین ترک جنگی چه داری بیاد = direct question in which کزین = 'of/from this', ترک جنگی = 'a warlike Turk' (ترک modified by جنگی), چه داری = 'what do you have/what do you recall' (چه = what; داری = present 'you have' used in reported/direct question), بیاد = 'to memory / in mind' (used with داری to mean 'do you recall'). The clause asks what memories or warlike deeds of this Turk come to mind.
segments.s665-g261: چنین داد پاسخ بدو مرد پیر = 'thus gave answer to him the old man' (چنین = thus, داد = gave (past), پاسخ = answer, بدو = to him; مرد پیر = the old man). Poetic SOV/SVO inversions place داد (verb) before the subject مرد پیر.
segments.s665-g262: که‌ای شاه گوینده و یادگیر introduces the quoted speech: که (introducing 'that' / the following words), ای شاه = 'O king' (vocative), گوینده و یادگیر = two nouns used as imperatival roles 'speaker and learner' (i.e. 'speak and learn' or 'be both speaker and listener/learner'). The short noun pair functions as exhortation addressed to the king.
segments.s665-g263: بدانگه کجا مادرت را ز چین = 'then/thereupon where (کجا) your mother (مادرت + object marker را) from China (ز چین) ...' بدانگه is a temporal adverb ('thereupon/then'), را marks مادرت as definite object. The clause asks for the place to which she was sent, with 'ز چین' marking origin.
segments.s665-g264: فرستاد خاقان به ایران‌زمین = 'the Khaghan sent (her) to the land of Iran' (فرستاد = sent, خاقان = Khaghan as subject, به ایران‌زمین = destination 'to Iran-land'). The verb precedes the subject here by poetic inversion.
segments.s665-g369: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing the reported clause. دادی is 2nd-person singular past of دادن 'you gave'; بدو is the clitic-like prepositional object 'to him' (به + او). بردع is the direct object 'a mantle / cloak'. و links on to the following noun phrase (اردبیل) so the clause flows into the next segment.
segments.s665-g370: یکی here functions as an emphatic/indefinite preface to the predicate ('became'), introducing the quality that follows. نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; گشت is the past of گشتن used here as 'became'. با introduces the instrumental/associative phrase 'with' (in this case means 'accompanied by'); کوس is 'war-drum' and خیل 'host/host of troops'.
segments.s665-g371: فرستاد is a 3rd-person past 'he sent' (subject is implicit in the narrative). و links two actions. بهرام را uses را as the definite object/topic marker ('Bahram' as the recipient/topic). مژده is 'good news / tidings' and داد is the past of دادن 'gave' (here 'gave the tidings to Bahram').
segments.s665-g372: سخنهای مهران is the noun phrase 'the words / speeches of Mehran'. برو here should be read as the fused poetic form بر او ('بر' + 'او') meaning 'upon him / to him' (so it is not the verb 'go'); because that sense is not listed in the provided senses, I mark it as -1. کرد is 'made / caused' (past of کردن) and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' used with کرد to mean 'caused [it] to be remembered' = 'reminded him'.
segments.s665-g373: جهانجوی پویان is a compound epithet ('world-seeking, Puyan') describing a person. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here indicating source or relation to the mantle). بردع is the same noun 'mantle / cloak'. برفت is a past form of رفتن 'he went / departed' (here implying he set off after the action); the printed senses given in the words list do not match this verbal sense, so the provided lemma senses do not perfectly reflect the usage.
segments.s665-g374: ز again 'from' or 'against' introducing the opponent. گردنکشان is an epithet meaning 'neck-breakers', i.e. rebels or fierce opponents. لشکری qualifies the action as military/army-related ('military' or 'an army'); برد here functions as 'victory' or 'a taking/winning' (a nominal/poetic usage). تفت in the word-list is given with senses related to burning/roasting; here the word most likely functions as a place-name or otherwise is not used with those listed senses, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.s665-g375: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the subject. تنگ 'narrow' modifies the place; اندر آمد is the compound 'came in / entered' (اندر = 'in/into' + آمد = 'came'). ز راه is an adverbial 'from the road / from the way' (ز = from, راه = road/way).
segments.s665-g376: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said' (used for royal speech or orders). تا here introduces the consequence/subordinate clause 'so that / in order that'. بار is 'load / baggage'; دادند is the past plural 'they gave' (here 'they loaded / they put the load'); شاه at the end is the topical/identifying word 'the king' (poetic inversion places the subject after the clause).

sentences.s665-s61: I will pass over this kingdom; a host is at sea and upon mountain and plain.
sentences.s665-s62: When the king read that letter, he withered at the sight of that countless host.
sentences.s665-s63: And thereupon Caesar came from Rome, and brought his army down into the land.
sentences.s665-s64: The army was Roman, numbering one hundred thousand — horsemen, warlike and renowned.
sentences.s665-s65: From the city that Nushin‑ravan captured — he was, by that name, called Caesar Nawan.
sentences.s665-s66: From every country an army came into the presence of the inner court, famed and foremost.
sentences.s665-s67: A force came by the Khazar road; because of them all the land and dwellings turned black.
sentences.s665-s68: The world‑wise Badal stood before (them); he was accompanied by his treasure and his army.
sentences.s665-s89: Our suffering came from the road of Khorasan, which will lay waste our army and treasure.
sentences.s665-s90: When the Turk comes from the Jihun into battle, we must not delay in carrying out this affair.

sentences.s665-s73: The army advanced as far as the Euphrates; no place for vegetation remained in that land.
sentences.s665-s74: When the good days grew dark, news of the army reached Bahramz.
sentences.s665-s75: When he heard the words of the informants, the once-vigorous king of the world withered.
sentences.s665-s76: He sent (messengers) and summoned the Iranians, and seated the people throughout the palaces.
sentences.s665-s37: On the road he saw a rose full of sour (unripe) grapes, and he ordered that the younger one run in.
sentences.s665-s38: He cut off some from that cluster and carried them, and gave them to the pavilion and entrusted them to his attendant.
sentences.s665-s39: His lord came at that moment, and to that man he said, 'O you of ill suspicion.'
sentences.s665-s40: You were not the guardian of this rose by toil; you gave neither a dinar as its price nor any treasure.
sentences.s665-s5: From a bad disposition comes something even worse; heed this — do not look toward a bad habit.
sentences.s665-s6: And after that his life was not happy; from anxiety a fever struck his heart.
sentences.s665-s7: You were proud for a year; for two months the nights were short and dark.
sentences.s665-s8: That city was cool and the air was clear; it would not have been right to pass from that place.
sentences.s665-s69: From Armenia as far as Ardabil his army was scattered, host after host.
sentences.s665-s70: From the plain came horsemen and spear-bearers; soldiers arrived in numbers beyond counting.
sentences.s665-s71: With Abbas and Hamzeh as the vanguard, horsemen and newly proud champions (rode at the front).
sentences.s665-s72: By pillage that land was laid waste, and it was saved when Hormuz himself sprang upon them with a cry.
sentences.s665-s113: Their commander stood before Khurad, who possessed royal glory and a throne and were endowed with justice.
sentences.s665-s114: When he came into Armenia with his army, the Khazar forces took to the road.
sentences.s665-s115: They slew many of them and also seized from that border many things.
sentences.s665-s116: When the news reached the king that Khurad had become victorious with his army.
sentences.s665-s77: He revealed a secret that had been hidden and told it to the renowned men of Iran.
sentences.s665-s78: That he had marshaled many hosts toward Iran; no one in the world now remembers this.
sentences.s665-s79: All the renowned men were left speechless and were driven from every kind of thought.
sentences.s665-s80: They said, 'O king, with counsel and wit, lend an ear to this matter.'
sentences.s665-s81: O wise king — we are the lesser; let us not count ourselves among the mobeds.
sentences.s665-s82: Consider, then, what the solution to the matter is; who, then, will be the guardian of our land?
sentences.s665-s83: Thus said the mobed: 'Who is to be his vizier? — O king, wise and receptive to learning.'
sentences.s665-s84: If a Khazar host should come to battle, do not seek a fight; do not tarry even for a moment.
sentences.s665-s21: The lord of that field came, and before the keeper he wailed piteously.
sentences.s665-s22: The keeper said to him, 'Whose horse is this that one must cry over its tail and ear?'
sentences.s665-s23: The lord said, 'The steed of King Parviz — it has no regard for the lowly.'
sentences.s665-s24: The keeper came to the ruler and told him what he had heard from the field.
sentences.s665-s109: When the messenger reached Caesar, he told him what he had heard from the King of Iran.
sentences.s665-s110: At that time the Roman king returned from the road and did not bring war into those borders and lands.
sentences.s665-s111: He selected an army of Iranians who vanished from sight even by day.
sentences.s665-s112: He sent them so that they would go to those lands and set foot within the Khazar frontier.
sentences.s665-s9: When the lapis-blue tent was hidden, a yellow ruby mountain appeared.
sentences.s665-s10: You raised a herald's cry: 'O renowned ones, endowed with farr (royal glory) and sense.'
sentences.s665-s11: If a slayer should be crushed, from that piercing suffering he will be distraught.
sentences.s665-s12: And if a horse goes into a cultivated field, someone will likewise go into the orchard (fruit-garden).
sentences.s665-s161: The world-seeker bore the epithet “Chubineh,” and his lineage was likewise from the champions.
sentences.s665-s162: When this man, a retainer of a small troop, came from some place to the king’s court.
sentences.s665-s163: He suddenly struck down Marin the Turk and scattered his entire army.
sentences.s665-s164: When he heard it, Shamr the Star said, “I have not seen anyone happier than the khaghan.”

sentences.s665-s86: I have no fear in my heart of the Arabs, for being seen by them brings harm.
sentences.s665-s87: For they are both snake‑eaters and lizards, and at times of war they have no battlefield (i.e. no real war‑prowess).
sentences.s665-s88: To you, the king of Saveh is nearer, and because of him our affairs are even darker.

sentences.s665-s15: For the realm you administered justice thus; you even won the peasant’s praise.
sentences.s665-s16: He had (or produced) a son whom he valued highly, a certain one who was scarcely inferior to the moon in appearance.
sentences.s665-s1: Hormoz understood that he would, unguided, shed blood while still alive.
sentences.s665-s2: He heard those fruitless words and sent Bahram to prison.
sentences.s665-s3: Another night, when the moon’s head rose above the mountain, he had him ruined by the prison's watchman.
sentences.s665-s4: That time-worn prudence did not remain in his house — that very guidance and even the mobed.
sentences.s665-s121: He has taken a seat in a corner and is alive; from hope in the world he has grown old and weak.
